> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**

> - **Topic:** Engineering Application of Optimization - Statement of an Optimization Problem - Classification, Review of Basic Calculus Concepts - Stationary Points

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Engineering Application of Optimization

**Optimization** is the act of obtaining the *best possible result* under given circumstances. In engineering design, construction, and maintenance, every decision-maker strives to achieve the *minimum cost, maximum profit, minimum weight, maximum reliability*, or any other optimal criterion consistent with the operational constraints.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal KTU Definition (2024 Scheme):**
> *Optimization is the mathematical discipline concerned with finding the values of decision variables in a defined domain that extremize (minimize or maximize) an objective function while satisfying a set of physical, geometric, or technological constraints.*

### 1.1.1 Conceptual Analogy — The "Hiker on a Mountain" Intuition

Imagine a **hiker lost in a foggy mountain range at night** who wants to reach the lowest valley (or the highest peak) as quickly as possible. The hiker cannot see the terrain but can feel the *slope* of the ground beneath his feet.

- The **terrain map** = the *objective function* $f(\mathbf{x})$.
- The **hiker's coordinates** (latitude, longitude, altitude) = the *design variables* $\mathbf{x} = (x_1, x_2, \dots, x_n)$.
- The **rocky cliffs and rivers** he must avoid = the *constraints*.
- The **rule "walk in the steepest downhill direction"** = the *gradient descent algorithm*.
- The **valley floor (zero slope)** = the *stationary point* (optimum).

Just as the hiker uses calculus (slope = 0 at the bottom) to find the valley, the engineer uses calculus-based conditions to locate the optimum design.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Optimization Matters in Engineering (KTU High-Yield Insight):**
> Modern engineering problems involve *trade-offs*. A structural engineer minimizes weight while maintaining strength; an electrical engineer minimizes power loss while ensuring voltage stability; a chemical engineer maximizes yield while respecting safety limits. Optimization provides the **mathematical backbone** for all such rational decision-making.

---

## 1.2 Statement of an Optimization Problem

A general optimization problem, in its standard *mathematical programming* form, is expressed as:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Find:} \quad & \mathbf{x} = (x_1, x_2, \dots, x_n) \\[4pt]
\text{To Optimize:} \quad & f(\mathbf{x}) \\[4pt]
\text{Subject to:} \quad & g_j(\mathbf{x}) \leq 0, \quad j = 1, 2, \dots, m \\[4pt]
& h_k(\mathbf{x}) = 0, \quad k = 1, 2, \dots, p \\[4pt]
& x_i^{(L)} \leq x_i \leq x_i^{(U)}, \quad i = 1, 2, \dots, n
\end{aligned}
$$

Where:
- $\mathbf{x}$ is the **vector of design variables**.
- $f(\mathbf{x})$ is the **objective function** (also called *criterion function*, *cost function*, or *merit function*).
- $g_j(\mathbf{x}) \leq 0$ are the **inequality constraints**.
- $h_k(\mathbf{x}) = 0$ are the **equality constraints**.
- $x_i^{(L)}$ and $x_i^{(U)}$ are the **side (bound) constraints** on individual variables.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Examiner's Note:**
> The phrase *Find the optimum of $f(\mathbf{x})$* is treated as equivalent to *Minimize $f(\mathbf{x})$* because any **maximization** problem can be converted to a minimization problem via the identity:
> $$\max_{\mathbf{x}} f(\mathbf{x}) = -\min_{\mathbf{x}} \left[-f(\mathbf{x})\right]$$
> Always state the conversion explicitly in your KTU answer scripts to earn full marks.

---

## 1.3 Classification of Optimization Problems

Optimization problems are broadly classified along **several independent dimensions**. KTU examiners frequently test this taxonomy (typically as a 3-mark Part A question).

### Classification Matrix

| **Basis of Classification** | **Category 1** | **Category 2** |
|:---|:---|:---|
| Nature of objective function & constraints | **Linear Programming (LP)** | **Nonlinear Programming (NLP)** |
| Nature of design variables | **Continuous (Real-valued)** | **Discrete (Integer Programming)** |
| Presence of constraints | **Unconstrained** | **Constrained** |
| Number of objective functions | **Single-Objective** | **Multi-Objective** |
| Static / Dynamic nature | **Static Optimization** | **Dynamic Optimization** |
| Deterministic / Stochastic data | **Deterministic** | **Stochastic (under uncertainty)** |
| Convexity of feasible region | **Convex Programming** | **Non-Convex Programming** |

### 1.3.1 Expanded Definitions (KTU Board-Ready)

1. **Linear Programming (LP):** Objective function and *all* constraints are *linear* in the design variables. Solved by Simplex, graphical, or interior-point methods.
2. **Nonlinear Programming (NLP):** At least one of the objective or constraint functions is *nonlinear*. Requires calculus-based or heuristic techniques (Kuhn-Tucker conditions, gradient methods, genetic algorithms).
3. **Geometric Programming (GP):** A special class of NLP where the objective and constraints are expressed as *posynomials*.
4. **Quadratic Programming (QP):** Objective is *quadratic* and constraints are *linear*.
5. **Integer Programming (IP):** Design variables restricted to *integer* values (e.g., number of machines, number of staff).
6. **Dynamic Programming (DP):** The problem is decomposed into a sequence of *stages* (Bellman's Principle of Optimality applies).
7. **Stochastic Programming:** Some parameters are *random variables* described by probability distributions.
8. **Multi-Objective Optimization:** Multiple conflicting objectives exist simultaneously. A *Pareto-optimal* front is sought rather than a single point.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Engineering Examples per Category:**
> - **LP** → Production mix in a chemical plant where profit per unit is constant.
> - **NLP** → Minimum weight design of a truss bridge.
> - **IP** → Optimal number of transformers to purchase.
> - **DP** → Shortest path routing in a network.
> - **Multi-Objective** → Design a vehicle to *minimize cost* AND *maximize safety rating*.

---

## 1.4 Review of Basic Calculus Concepts — Stationary Points

A **stationary point** of a differentiable function $f(x)$ is a point where the *first derivative equals zero* (or is undefined).

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Stationary Point Condition:} \quad f'(x) = 0
\end{aligned}
$$

Geometrically, this corresponds to a **horizontal tangent** on the curve $y = f(x)$. Stationary points may be classified as:
- **Local Minimum** (valley)
- **Local Maximum** (peak)
- **Inflection Point** (saddle in 1-D)

The **second derivative test** distinguishes them:

$$
\begin{aligned}
& f''(x^*) > 0 \implies \text{Local Minimum at } x^* \\[4pt]
& f''(x^*) < 0 \implies \text{Local Maximum at } x^* \\[4pt]
& f''(x^*) = 0 \implies \text{Test Inconclusive (Higher-order test or graphical inspection required)}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Trap Alert:**
> A stationary point is **NOT always an optimum**. A point where $f'(x) = 0$ could merely be a *point of inflection* with a horizontal tangent (e.g., $f(x) = x^3$ at $x = 0$). Always confirm with the second-derivative test *and* graphical verification.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Components of an Optimization Problem — Decomposition

Every well-posed engineering optimization problem comprises **five essential components**. KTU examiners award marks for *correctly identifying all five* in the answer script.

### Component 1: Design Variables ($\mathbf{x}$)
The **independent, controllable parameters** that the designer is free to choose. These are the *knobs* we turn to improve the design.
- Notation: $\mathbf{x} = [x_1, x_2, \dots, x_n]^T \in \mathbb{R}^n$
- Example: thickness of a beam, diameter of a pipe, number of hours of machine operation.

### Component 2: Objective Function ($f(\mathbf{x})$)
A **scalar measure of performance** that quantifies "goodness" of a design. Must be a *single real number* for a given $\mathbf{x}$.
- **Minimize** cost, weight, time, error, emission.
- **Maximize** profit, strength, efficiency, reliability, throughput.

### Component 3: Constraints ($g_j, h_k$)
**Restrictions imposed** by physics, geometry, manufacturing, safety codes, regulations, or available resources.
- *Inequality* constraints: $g_j(\mathbf{x}) \leq 0$ (e.g., stress $\leq$ allowable stress).
- *Equality* constraints: $h_k(\mathbf{x}) = 0$ (e.g., force equilibrium, energy balance).

### Component 4: Feasible Region ($S$)
The **set of all admissible designs** that satisfy *every* constraint simultaneously:
$$
\begin{aligned}
S = \left\{ \mathbf{x} \in \mathbb{R}^n \,\big\vert\, g_j(\mathbf{x}) \leq 0, \; h_k(\mathbf{x}) = 0, \; x_i^{(L)} \leq x_i \leq x_i^{(U)} \right\}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Component 5: Optimum Solution ($\mathbf{x}^*$)
A point $\mathbf{x}^* \in S$ is the **global optimum** if no other feasible point yields a better objective value. It is a **local optimum** if it is better than all *nearby* feasible points.

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal KTU Definitions (Examinable):**
> - **Global Minimum:** $f(\mathbf{x}^*) \leq f(\mathbf{x})$ for **all** $\mathbf{x} \in S$.
> - **Strict Global Minimum:** $f(\mathbf{x}^*) < f(\mathbf{x})$ for all $\mathbf{x} \in S$, $\mathbf{x} \neq \mathbf{x}^*$.
> - **Local Minimum:** There exists an $\varepsilon > 0$ such that $f(\mathbf{x}^*) \leq f(\mathbf{x})$ for **all** $\mathbf{x} \in S \cap \{ \mathbf{x} : \Vert \mathbf{x} - \mathbf{x}^* \Vert < \varepsilon \}$.

---

## 2.2 Mathematical Preliminaries — Calculus Tools Used Throughout the Course

The KTU 2024 Module 1 syllabus requires fluency with the following calculus instruments. Each is summarized in board-ready notation below.

### 2.2.1 Taylor Series Expansion (First-Order and Second-Order)

For a *univariate*, *twice continuously differentiable* function $f(x)$ expanded about the point $x^*$:
$$
\begin{aligned}
f(x) \approx f(x^*) + f'(x^*)(x - x^*) + \frac{1}{2} f''(x^*)(x - x^*)^2 + \dots
\end{aligned}
$$

For a *multivariate* function $f(\mathbf{x})$ expanded about $\mathbf{x}^*$:
$$
\begin{aligned}
f(\mathbf{x}) \approx f(\mathbf{x}^*) + \nabla f(\mathbf{x}^*)^T (\mathbf{x} - \mathbf{x}^*) + \frac{1}{2} (\mathbf{x} - \mathbf{x}^*)^T \nabla^2 f(\mathbf{x}^*) (\mathbf{x} - \mathbf{x}^*)
\end{aligned}
$$

where $\nabla f$ is the **gradient vector** and $\nabla^2 f$ is the **Hessian matrix**.

### 2.2.2 The Gradient Vector

$$
\begin{aligned}
\nabla f(\mathbf{x}) = \begin{bmatrix} \dfrac{\partial f}{\partial x_1} \\[8pt] \dfrac{\partial f}{\partial x_2} \\[4pt] \vdots \\[4pt] \dfrac{\partial f}{\partial x_n} \end{bmatrix}
\end{aligned}
$$

Geometric interpretation: $\nabla f$ points in the direction of **steepest ascent** of $f$.

### 2.2.3 The Hessian Matrix

$$
\begin{aligned}
\nabla^2 f(\mathbf{x}) = \mathbf{H}(\mathbf{x}) = \begin{bmatrix}
\dfrac{\partial^2 f}{\partial x_1^2} & \dfrac{\partial^2 f}{\partial x_1 \partial x_2} & \cdots & \dfrac{\partial^2 f}{\partial x_1 \partial x_n} \\[10pt]
\dfrac{\partial^2 f}{\partial x_2 \partial x_1} & \dfrac{\partial^2 f}{\partial x_2^2} & \cdots & \dfrac{\partial^2 f}{\partial x_2 \partial x_n} \\[6pt]
\vdots & \vdots & \ddots & \vdots \\[6pt]
\dfrac{\partial^2 f}{\partial x_n \partial x_1} & \dfrac{\partial^2 f}{\partial x_n \partial x_2} & \cdots & \dfrac{\partial^2 f}{\partial x_n^2}
\end{bmatrix}
\end{aligned}
$$

The Hessian is **symmetric** when $f \in C^2$ (twice continuously differentiable) due to equality of mixed partial derivatives (Schwarz theorem).

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Module 1)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table consolidates every formula you must memorize for Module 1. Reproduce it on your *last revision sheet* the night before the KTU ESE.

| **#** | **Concept** | **Mathematical Statement** | **Significance / Condition** |
|:---:|:---|:---|:---|
| 1 | Stationary Point (1-D) | $f'(x^*) = 0$ | Necessary condition for *interior* extremum |
| 2 | Minimum Test (1-D) | $f'(x^*) = 0, \; f''(x^*) > 0$ | Sufficient condition for local min |
| 3 | Maximum Test (1-D) | $f'(x^*) = 0, \; f''(x^*) < 0$ | Sufficient condition for local max |
| 4 | Inconclusive (1-D) | $f'(x^*) = 0, \; f''(x^*) = 0$ | Higher-order or graphical test |
| 5 | Stationary Point ($n$-D) | $\nabla f(\mathbf{x}^*) = \mathbf{0}$ | All first partials vanish simultaneously |
| 6 | Hessian Determinant | $D = \vert \mathbf{H}(\mathbf{x}^*) \vert$ | Combined with leading principal minors |
| 7 | Min Test ($n$-D) | $\nabla f = \mathbf{0}$, $\mathbf{H}$ positive definite | All leading principal minors $> 0$ |
| 8 | Max Test ($n$-D) | $\nabla f = \mathbf{0}$, $\mathbf{H}$ negative definite | All leading principal minors alternate in sign |
| 9 | Saddle Point ($n$-D) | $\nabla f = \mathbf{0}$, $\mathbf{H}$ indefinite | Neither min nor max |
| 10 | Positive Definiteness | $\mathbf{y}^T \mathbf{H} \mathbf{y} > 0 \;\; \forall \, \mathbf{y} \neq \mathbf{0}$ | Eigenvalues all $> 0$ |
| 11 | Max-to-Min Conversion | $\max f = -\min(-f)$ | Standard reduction trick |
| 12 | Taylor Remainder (2nd Order) | $R_2 = \frac{1}{2}(\mathbf{x} - \mathbf{x}^*)^T \mathbf{H}(\boldsymbol{\xi})(\mathbf{x} - \mathbf{x}^*)$ | Local quadratic model |
| 13 | Gradient Direction | $\mathbf{d} = -\nabla f$ | Direction of steepest descent |
| 14 | Gradient Magnitude | $\Vert \nabla f \Vert = \sqrt{\sum \left(\frac{\partial f}{\partial x_i}\right)^2}$ | Measure of slope intensity |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:**
> - In **structural optimization**, $\nabla f = \mathbf{0}$ yields the equilibrium topology.
> - In **machine learning**, gradient descent ($\mathbf{x}_{k+1} = \mathbf{x}_k - \alpha_k \nabla f$) uses the gradient direction.
> - In **process control**, the Hessian governs the *curvature* of the response surface and dictates Newton-step size.

---

## 2.4 Positive Definiteness — Principal Minor Test

For a symmetric $n \times n$ matrix $\mathbf{H}$, the **leading principal minors** are:
$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta_1 = h_{11}, \quad \Delta_2 = \begin{vmatrix} h_{11} & h_{12} \\ h_{12} & h_{22} \end{vmatrix}, \quad \Delta_3 = \begin{vmatrix} h_{11} & h_{12} & h_{13} \\ h_{12} & h_{22} & h_{23} \\ h_{13} & h_{23} & h_{33} \end{vmatrix}, \dots
\end{aligned}
$$

**Sylvester's Criterion (for Positive Definiteness):**
$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta_k > 0 \;\; \text{for all} \; k = 1, 2, \dots, n
\end{aligned}
$$

**For Negative Definiteness:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
(-1)^k \Delta_k > 0 \;\; \text{for all} \; k = 1, 2, \dots, n
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Frequently Confused Point:**
> - *Positive definite* $\Rightarrow$ **Strict local minimum**.
> - *Positive semi-definite* ($\Delta_k \geq 0$, at least one $= 0$) $\Rightarrow$ inconclusive.
> - *Negative definite* $\Rightarrow$ **Strict local maximum**.
> - *Indefinite* $\Rightarrow$ **Saddle point**.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation: The First-Order Necessary Condition (FONC) for Unconstrained Optima

### 3.1.1 Univariate Case — Complete Proof

**Theorem:** If $f(x)$ is differentiable at an interior local minimum $x^*$, then $f'(x^*) = 0$.

**Proof using Taylor Series:**
Let $f: \mathbb{R} \to \mathbb{R}$ be differentiable at $x^*$ with $x^*$ a local minimum. Consider an arbitrary perturbation $\delta$ (positive or negative). The Taylor expansion about $x^*$ is:
$$
\begin{aligned}
f(x^* + \delta) = f(x^*) + f'(x^*) \, \delta + \frac{1}{2} f''(\xi) \, \delta^2
\end{aligned}$$

Since $x^*$ is a local minimum, we must have $f(x^* + \delta) \geq f(x^*)$ for sufficiently small $\vert \delta \vert$:
$$
\begin{aligned}
f(x^*) + f'(x^*) \, \delta + \frac{1}{2} f''(\xi) \, \delta^2 \;\geq\; f(x^*)
\end{aligned}$$

Subtracting $f(x^*)$ from both sides:
$$
\begin{aligned}
f'(x^*) \, \delta + \frac{1}{2} f''(\xi) \, \delta^2 \;\geq\; 0
\end{aligned}$$

For *sufficiently small* $\delta$, the quadratic term $\frac{1}{2} f''(\xi) \delta^2$ becomes negligible compared to the linear term $f'(x^*) \delta$. The sign of the LHS is then governed by $f'(x^*) \delta$. For this inequality to hold for *both* signs of $\delta$, we require:
$$
\begin{aligned}
f'(x^*) = 0
\end{aligned}
$$

$\blacksquare$

### 3.1.2 Multivariate Case — Generalization

**Theorem:** If $f: \mathbb{R}^n \to \mathbb{R}$ is differentiable at an interior local minimum $\mathbf{x}^*$, then $\nabla f(\mathbf{x}^*) = \mathbf{0}$.

**Proof Sketch:**
Consider the perturbed point $\mathbf{x} = \mathbf{x}^* + \delta \mathbf{e}_j$, where $\mathbf{e}_j$ is the $j$-th unit vector. The 1-D restriction $g_j(\delta) = f(\mathbf{x}^* + \delta \mathbf{e}_j)$ has a local minimum at $\delta = 0$. By the 1-D FONC, $g_j'(0) = 0$. But $g_j'(0) = \dfrac{\partial f}{\partial x_j}(\mathbf{x}^*)$ by the chain rule. Since this holds for every $j = 1, 2, \dots, n$:
$$
\begin{aligned}
\nabla f(\mathbf{x}^*) = \begin{bmatrix} \dfrac{\partial f}{\partial x_1}(\mathbf{x}^*) \\[8pt] \dfrac{\partial f}{\partial x_2}(\mathbf{x}^*) \\[4pt] \vdots \\[4pt] \dfrac{\partial f}{\partial x_n}(\mathbf{x}^*) \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\[4pt] 0 \\[4pt] \vdots \\[4pt] 0 \end{bmatrix} = \mathbf{0}
\end{aligned}
$$

$\blacksquare$

---

## 3.2 Derivation: The Second-Order Sufficient Condition (SOSC)

**Theorem:** If $\nabla f(\mathbf{x}^*) = \mathbf{0}$ and $\mathbf{H}(\mathbf{x}^*)$ is *positive definite*, then $\mathbf{x}^*$ is a **strict local minimum** of $f$.

**Proof:**
From the second-order Taylor expansion:
$$
\begin{aligned}
f(\mathbf{x}) = f(\mathbf{x}^*) + \nabla f(\mathbf{x}^*)^T (\mathbf{x} - \mathbf{x}^*) + \frac{1}{2} (\mathbf{x} - \mathbf{x}^*)^T \mathbf{H}(\mathbf{x}^*) (\mathbf{x} - \mathbf{x}^*) + o(\Vert \mathbf{x} - \mathbf{x}^* \Vert^2)
\end{aligned}
$$

Since $\nabla f(\mathbf{x}^*) = \mathbf{0}$, the linear term vanishes:
$$
\begin{aligned}
f(\mathbf{x}) = f(\mathbf{x}^*) + \frac{1}{2} (\mathbf{x} - \mathbf{x}^*)^T \mathbf{H}(\mathbf{x}^*) (\mathbf{x} - \mathbf{x}^*) + o(\Vert \mathbf{x} - \mathbf{x}^* \Vert^2)
\end{aligned}
$$

For $\mathbf{x}$ close to $\mathbf{x}^*$, the quadratic form dominates the higher-order term $o(\Vert \mathbf{x} - \mathbf{x}^* \Vert^2)$. If $\mathbf{H}(\mathbf{x}^*)$ is positive definite, then:
$$
\begin{aligned}
(\mathbf{x} - \mathbf{x}^*)^T \mathbf{H}(\mathbf{x}^*) (\mathbf{x} - \mathbf{x}^*) > 0 \quad \forall \, \mathbf{x} \neq \mathbf{x}^*
\end{aligned}
$$

Therefore $f(\mathbf{x}) > f(\mathbf{x}^*)$ in a sufficiently small neighborhood, establishing $\mathbf{x}^*$ as a *strict local minimum*.

$\blacksquare$

---

## 3.3 Worked Example 1 — Univariate Stationary Point Identification

**Problem:** Find and classify the stationary points of $f(x) = x^3 - 6x^2 + 9x + 2$.

### Step 1: Compute the first derivative
$$
\begin{aligned}
f(x) &= x^3 - 6x^2 + 9x + 2 \\[4pt]
f'(x) &= 3x^2 - 12x + 9
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 2: Set $f'(x) = 0$ and solve
$$
\begin{aligned}
3x^2 - 12x + 9 &= 0 \\[4pt]
x^2 - 4x + 3 &= 0 \\[4pt]
(x - 1)(x - 3) &= 0 \\[4pt]
\therefore \;\; x_1 = 1, \quad x_2 = 3
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3: Compute the second derivative
$$
\begin{aligned}
f''(x) = 6x - 12
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4: Apply the second-derivative test
$$
\begin{aligned}
f''(1) &= 6(1) - 12 = -6 < 0 \implies \text{Local MAXIMUM at } x = 1 \\[4pt]
f''(3) &= 6(3) - 12 = +6 > 0 \implies \text{Local MINIMUM at } x = 3
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 5: Compute the function values
$$
\begin{aligned}
f(1) &= (1)^3 - 6(1)^2 + 9(1) + 2 = 1 - 6 + 9 + 2 = 6 \\[4pt]
f(3) &= (3)^3 - 6(3)^2 + 9(3) + 2 = 27 - 54 + 27 + 2 = 2
\end{aligned}
$$

### Final Answer
- **Local Maximum:** $(1, 6)$
- **Local Minimum:** $(3, 2)$

---

## 3.4 Worked Example 2 — Bivariate Optimum via Hessian Test

**Problem:** Find the stationary point of $f(x, y) = x^2 + y^2 - 4x - 6y + 13$ and classify it.

### Step 1: First-order partial derivatives
$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{\partial f}{\partial x} &= 2x - 4 \\[4pt]
\frac{\partial f}{\partial y} &= 2y - 6
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 2: Set gradient to zero
$$
\begin{aligned}
\nabla f = \mathbf{0} \implies \begin{cases} 2x - 4 = 0 \implies x = 2 \\ 2y - 6 = 0 \implies y = 3 \end{cases}
\end{aligned}
$$

Stationary point: $(x^*, y^*) = (2, 3)$.

### Step 3: Construct the Hessian matrix
$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{\partial^2 f}{\partial x^2} &= 2 \\[4pt]
\frac{\partial^2 f}{\partial y^2} &= 2 \\[4pt]
\frac{\partial^2 f}{\partial x \, \partial y} &= \frac{\partial^2 f}{\partial y \, \partial x} = 0 \\[8pt]
\mathbf{H} &= \begin{bmatrix} 2 & 0 \\ 0 & 2 \end{bmatrix}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4: Apply Sylvester's criterion
$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta_1 &= 2 > 0 \\[4pt]
\Delta_2 &= \begin{vmatrix} 2 & 0 \\ 0 & 2 \end{vmatrix} = 4 > 0
\end{aligned}
$$

Both leading principal minors are *positive* $\Rightarrow$ **Positive definite** $\Rightarrow$ **Strict local minimum**.

### Step 5: Compute the minimum value
$$
\begin{aligned}
f(2, 3) = (2)^2 + (3)^2 - 4(2) - 6(3) + 13 = 4 + 9 - 8 - 18 + 13 = 0
\end{aligned}
$$

### Final Answer
The function $f(x, y)$ attains a **strict local minimum** of $\mathbf{0}$ at $(2, 3)$.

---

## 3.5 Worked Example 3 — Saddle Point Identification

**Problem:** Find and classify all stationary points of $f(x, y) = x^2 - y^2$.

### Step 1: First partials
$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{\partial f}{\partial x} &= 2x \\[4pt]
\frac{\partial f}{\partial y} &= -2y
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 2: Gradient zero
$$
\begin{aligned}
\nabla f = \mathbf{0} \implies x = 0, \;\; y = 0
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3: Hessian
$$
\begin{aligned}
\mathbf{H} = \begin{bmatrix} 2 & 0 \\ 0 & -2 \end{bmatrix}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4: Test
$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta_1 &= 2 > 0 \\[4pt]
\Delta_2 &= (2)(-2) - 0 = -4 < 0
\end{aligned}
$$

Since $\Delta_2 < 0$, the Hessian is **indefinite** $\Rightarrow$ **Saddle point** at $(0, 0)$.

### Verification
Along $y = 0$ (x-axis): $f(x, 0) = x^2 \geq 0$ (minimum direction).
Along $x = 0$ (y-axis): $f(0, y) = -y^2 \leq 0$ (maximum direction).
This confirms the saddle nature of the origin.

---

## 3.6 Analytical Matrix — Quick-Reference Classification Table

| **Nature of Hessian** | **Sign of $\Delta_1$** | **Sign of $\Delta_2$** | **Geometric Name** | **Optimization Verdict** |
|:---|:---:|:---:|:---|:---|
| Positive Definite | $> 0$ | $> 0$ | Elliptic paraboloid (cup) | **Strict local minimum** |
| Negative Definite | $< 0$ | $> 0$ | Inverted cup | **Strict local maximum** |
| Indefinite | $> 0$ | $< 0$ | Hyperbolic paraboloid (saddle) | **Saddle point** |
| Positive Semi-definite | $> 0$ | $= 0$ | Cylindrical valley | **Inconclusive** |
| Negative Semi-definite | $< 0$ | $= 0$ | Cylindrical ridge | **Inconclusive** |

> [!NOTE]
> This 2-variable matrix is the **most frequently tested artefact** in KTU Module 1 questions worth 7-14 marks.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Anatomy of an Optimization Problem

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["ENGINEERING OPTIMIZATION PROBLEM"]:::root --> B["Design Variables x1 to xn"]
    A --> C["Objective Function f of x"]
    A --> D["Constraints gj of x and hk of x"]
    A --> E["Feasible Region S"]
    A --> F["Optimal Solution x star"]

    B --> B1["Continuous or Discrete"]

    C --> C1["Minimize Cost or Weight"]
    C --> C2["Maximize Profit or Strength"]

    D --> D3["Side or Bound Constraints"]

    E --> E1["Intersection of all Constraint Sets"]
    E --> E2["May be Empty Infeasible"]

    F --> F2["Local Optimum"]
    F --> F3["Stationary Point Check"]

    F3 --> G1["FONC: Gradient equals zero"]
    F3 --> G2["SOSC: Hessian Definite"]

```

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**State the standard form of an optimization problem. List its essential components.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
The standard form is:
$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Minimize} \quad & f(\mathbf{x}) \\
\text{Subject to} \quad & g_j(\mathbf{x}) \leq 0, \quad j = 1, \dots, m \\
& h_k(\mathbf{x}) = 0, \quad k = 1, \dots, p \\
& x_i^{(L)} \leq x_i \leq x_i^{(U)}, \quad i = 1, \dots, n
\end{aligned}
$$

**Essential Components:**
1. **Design Variables** — $\mathbf{x} = (x_1, \dots, x_n)$, the controllable parameters.
2. **Objective Function** — $f(\mathbf{x})$, the scalar performance measure.
3. **Constraints** — Inequalities $g_j$ and equalities $h_k$.
4. **Bounds (Side Constraints)** — Upper and lower limits on each variable.
5. **Feasible Region** — The set $S$ of all admissible $\mathbf{x}$ satisfying every constraint.

**Mark Allocation:** 1 mark for the formulation, 2 marks for the five components. **(3/3)**

---

### Question A2 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Define a stationary point. Using the second-derivative test, explain how a stationary point of a univariate function $f(x)$ is classified.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
A **stationary point** $x^*$ of a differentiable function $f(x)$ is a point where $f'(x^*) = 0$, i.e., the tangent to the curve is horizontal.

**Classification via Second-Derivative Test:**
1. If $f'(x^*) = 0$ and $f''(x^*) > 0 \Rightarrow$ **Local (relative) minimum** — the curve is concave-up like a cup.
2. If $f'(x^*) = 0$ and $f''(x^*) < 0 \Rightarrow$ **Local (relative) maximum** — the curve is concave-down like an inverted cup.
3. If $f'(x^*) = 0$ and $f''(x^*) = 0 \Rightarrow$ **Test is inconclusive** — the point may be a minimum, maximum, or inflection. A higher-order derivative test or graphical inspection is required.

**Mark Allocation:** 1 mark for the definition, 2 marks for the three cases. **(3/3)**

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question B1 (Choice A) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Classify optimization problems based on (i) the nature of the objective function and constraints, (ii) the nature of the design variables, (iii) the number of objective functions, and (iv) the static/dynamic nature of the problem. Give **one engineering example** for each category.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Find and classify all stationary points of $f(x) = 2x^3 - 9x^2 + 12x + 5$.

---

### Model Solution for Question B1

#### Part (a) — Classification with Examples [7 Marks]

**1. Nature of Objective Function & Constraints:**

| Type | Definition | Engineering Example |
|:---|:---|:---|
| **Linear Programming (LP)** | Both $f$ and all constraints are linear in $\mathbf{x}$ | Production mix in a refinery to maximize profit |
| **Nonlinear Programming (NLP)** | At least one of $f$ or constraints is nonlinear | Minimum weight design of a truss structure |

**2. Nature of Design Variables:**

| Type | Definition | Engineering Example |
|:---|:---|:---|
| **Continuous** | Variables take real values in a range | Diameter of a shaft |
| **Discrete (Integer)** | Variables restricted to integers | Number of pumps to install |

**3. Number of Objective Functions:**

| Type | Definition | Engineering Example |
|:---|:---|:---|
| **Single-Objective** | One scalar criterion to optimize | Minimum cost design |
| **Multi-Objective** | Multiple conflicting criteria | Design of a vehicle: minimum cost AND maximum safety |

**4. Static / Dynamic Nature:**

| Type | Definition | Engineering Example |
|:---|:---|:---|
| **Static Optimization** | Decisions are time-independent | Optimal cross-section of a column |
| **Dynamic Optimization** | Decisions vary with time | Optimal trajectory of a spacecraft |

**(1 mark per table, total 4 marks; 0.75 marks each example, total 3 marks = 7 marks)**

---

#### Part (b) — Stationary Point Identification [7 Marks]

**Given:** $f(x) = 2x^3 - 9x^2 + 12x + 5$.

**Step 1: First derivative**
$$
\begin{aligned}
f'(x) = 6x^2 - 18x + 12 = 6(x^2 - 3x + 2) = 6(x - 1)(x - 2)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Stationary points** (set $f'(x) = 0$)
$$
\begin{aligned}
(x - 1)(x - 2) = 0 \implies x_1 = 1, \quad x_2 = 2
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Second derivative**
$$
\begin{aligned}
f''(x) = 12x - 18
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: Second-derivative test**
$$
\begin{aligned}
f''(1) = 12(1) - 18 = -6 < 0 \implies \text{Local MAXIMUM at } x = 1 \\[4pt]
f''(2) = 12(2) - 18 = +6 > 0 \implies \text{Local MINIMUM at } x = 2
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5: Function values**
$$
\begin{aligned}
f(1) &= 2(1) - 9(1) + 12(1) + 5 = 2 - 9 + 12 + 5 = 10 \\[4pt]
f(2) &= 2(8) - 9(4) + 12(2) + 5 = 16 - 36 + 24 + 5 = 9
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final Classification:**
- **Local Maximum** at $(1, \; 10)$ with value $f(1) = 10$.
- **Local Minimum** at $(2, \; 9)$ with value $f(2) = 9$.

**Mark Allocation:** 2 marks for finding stationary points; 2 marks for second derivative and test; 2 marks for function values; 1 mark for correct classification. **(7/7)**

---

### Question B2 (Choice B) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** With a neat sketch, explain the geometric meaning of the gradient vector and the Hessian matrix. How are they used to identify local minima, maxima, and saddle points of a multivariate function?

**(b) [7 Marks]** Find the stationary point of $f(x, y) = x^3 + y^3 - 3xy$ and classify it using the Hessian test.

---

### Model Solution for Question B2

#### Part (a) — Geometric Interpretation of Gradient & Hessian [7 Marks]

**1. Geometric Meaning of the Gradient Vector $\nabla f$:**

The gradient $\nabla f(\mathbf{x}_0)$ at any point $\mathbf{x}_0$ is a vector that:
- **Points in the direction of steepest ascent** of $f$ at $\mathbf{x}_0$.
- **Its magnitude** $\Vert \nabla f \Vert$ equals the **maximum rate of change** of $f$ at $\mathbf{x}_0$.
- **Is perpendicular (normal)** to the *level curve* (or level surface in $n$-D) $f(\mathbf{x}) = \text{const}$ passing through $\mathbf{x}_0$.

For the function $f(x, y) = x^2 + y^2$, the level curves are concentric circles and $\nabla f = (2x, 2y)$ points radially outward — directly away from the origin, which is the minimum.

**2. Geometric Meaning of the Hessian Matrix $\mathbf{H}$:**

The Hessian is a **second-order descriptor of curvature**:
- Its **eigenvalues** measure the principal curvatures along orthogonal directions.
- If $\mathbf{H}$ is positive definite, the level surfaces are *ellipsoidal* (cup-shaped) ⇒ local **minimum**.
- If $\mathbf{H}$ is negative definite, the level surfaces are *inverted ellipsoidal* (cap-shaped) ⇒ local **maximum**.
- If $\mathbf{H}$ is indefinite, the level surfaces are *hyperbolic* (saddle-shaped) ⇒ **saddle point**.

**3. Classification Procedure:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Step 1:} \quad & \nabla f(\mathbf{x}^*) = \mathbf{0} \\[4pt]
\text{Step 2:} \quad & \text{Compute } \mathbf{H}(\mathbf{x}^*) \\[4pt]
\text{Step 3:} \quad & \text{Apply Sylvester's Criterion using leading principal minors } \Delta_k
\end{aligned}
$$

| Criterion | Conclusion |
|:---|:---|
| All $\Delta_k > 0$ | Positive Definite $\Rightarrow$ **Local Min** |
| $(-1)^k \Delta_k > 0 \;\; \forall k$ | Negative Definite $\Rightarrow$ **Local Max** |
| Some $\Delta_k < 0$ | Indefinite $\Rightarrow$ **Saddle Point** |

**Mark Allocation:** 2 marks for gradient geometry, 2 marks for Hessian geometry, 3 marks for classification logic. **(7/7)**

---

#### Part (b) — Bivariate Optimization with Hessian Test [7 Marks]

**Given:** $f(x, y) = x^3 + y^3 - 3xy$.

**Step 1: First-order partial derivatives**
$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{\partial f}{\partial x} &= 3x^2 - 3y \\[4pt]
\frac{\partial f}{\partial y} &= 3y^2 - 3x
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Solve $\nabla f = \mathbf{0}$**
$$
\begin{aligned}
3x^2 - 3y &= 0 \implies y = x^2 \\[4pt]
3y^2 - 3x &= 0 \implies x = y^2
\end{aligned}
$$

Substituting $y = x^2$ into $x = y^2$:
$$
\begin{aligned}
x = (x^2)^2 = x^4 \implies x^4 - x = 0 \implies x(x^3 - 1) = 0
\end{aligned}
$$

This gives $x = 0$ or $x = 1$.

Corresponding $y$ values:
- $x = 0 \implies y = 0^2 = 0$ → Stationary point **$P_1 = (0, 0)$**.
- $x = 1 \implies y = 1^2 = 1$ → Stationary point **$P_2 = (1, 1)$**.

**Step 3: Hessian matrix**
$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{\partial^2 f}{\partial x^2} &= 6x \\[4pt]
\frac{\partial^2 f}{\partial y^2} &= 6y \\[4pt]
\frac{\partial^2 f}{\partial x \partial y} &= \frac{\partial^2 f}{\partial y \partial x} = -3 \\[8pt]
\mathbf{H}(x, y) &= \begin{bmatrix} 6x & -3 \\ -3 & 6y \end{bmatrix}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: Classify $P_1 = (0, 0)$**
$$
\begin{aligned}
\mathbf{H}(0, 0) &= \begin{bmatrix} 0 & -3 \\ -3 & 0 \end{bmatrix} \\[4pt]
\Delta_1 &= 0 \\[4pt]
\Delta_2 &= (0)(0) - (-3)(-3) = -9 < 0
\end{aligned}
$$

Since $\Delta_2 < 0$, the Hessian is **indefinite** ⇒ **Saddle Point** at $(0, 0)$.

**Step 5: Classify $P_2 = (1, 1)$**
$$
\begin{aligned}
\mathbf{H}(1, 1) &= \begin{bmatrix} 6 & -3 \\ -3 & 6 \end{bmatrix} \\[4pt]
\Delta_1 &= 6 > 0 \\[4pt]
\Delta_2 &= (6)(6) - (-3)(-3) = 36 - 9 = 27 > 0
\end{aligned}
$$

Since both $\Delta_1 > 0$ and $\Delta_2 > 0$, the Hessian is **positive definite** ⇒ **Local Minimum** at $(1, 1)$.

**Step 6: Minimum value**
$$
\begin{aligned}
f(1, 1) = (1)^3 + (1)^3 - 3(1)(1) = 1 + 1 - 3 = -1
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final Classification:**
- **Saddle Point** at $(0, 0)$.
- **Local Minimum** at $(1, 1)$ with $f(1, 1) = -1$.

**Mark Allocation:** 2 marks for partials and solving gradient; 2 marks for Hessian construction; 2 marks for Sylvester test; 1 mark for function value and final verdict. **(7/7)**

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning & Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost 2-4 Marks in KTU ESE:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to check second derivative sign** — A student writes "stationary point at $x = 2$" and stops. Always apply $f''(x^*)$ test for full marks.
> 2. **Confusing *necessary* and *sufficient* conditions** — $f'(x^*) = 0$ is NECESSARY but not sufficient for a minimum. State both N and S conditions explicitly.
> 3. **Sign errors in cross-partials** — When constructing $\mathbf{H}$, always write both $\frac{\partial^2 f}{\partial x \partial y}$ and $\frac{\partial^2 f}{\partial y \partial x}$. They are equal (Schwarz theorem) only when $f \in C^2$.
> 4. **Forgetting the conversion $\max f = -\min(-f)$** — Always check the problem statement. If asked to *maximize* revenue, you must convert to a minimization form before proceeding.
> 5. **Computing only $\Delta_n$ instead of all leading principal minors** — Sylvester's criterion requires *all* $\Delta_1, \Delta_2, \dots, \Delta_n$, not just the determinant.
> 6. **Stating "saddle point" without specifying which direction is min and which is max** — Always add a one-line geometric or algebraic verification (e.g., "along $y = 0$, $f = x^3 \leq 0$ for $x < 0$").
> 7. **Not mentioning domain or feasibility** — Even for unconstrained problems, briefly state the domain (e.g., $x \in \mathbb{R}$) and that the stationary point is an *interior* point.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Module 1 Rapid-Revision Checklist (Print & Pin):**

- [ ] **Optimization** = choosing the *best* design among feasible alternatives.
- [ ] The standard form has **objective** + **inequality constraints** + **equality constraints** + **bounds**.
- [ ] **Max-to-Min conversion**: $\max f = -\min(-f)$.
- [ ] **Five components** of every optimization problem: Variables, Objective, Constraints, Feasible Region, Optimum.
- [ ] **Classification axes**: Linear/Nonlinear, Continuous/Discrete, Constrained/Unconstrained, Single/Multi-objective, Static/Dynamic, Deterministic/Stochastic.
- [ ] **Stationary point** (1-D): $f'(x^*) = 0$ — first-order necessary condition (FONC).
- [ ] **Second-derivative test** (1-D): $f''(x^*) > 0$ → min; $f''(x^*) < 0$ → max; $f''(x^*) = 0$ → inconclusive.
- [ ] **Gradient vector** $\nabla f$: points in the direction of steepest ascent; vanishes at stationary points.
- [ ] **Hessian matrix** $\mathbf{H}$: symmetric matrix of second partial derivatives; governs curvature.
- [ ] **Sylvester's Criterion** (Positive Definite): all leading principal minors $\Delta_k > 0$ for $k = 1, \dots, n$ → local **minimum**.
- [ ] **Sylvester's Criterion** (Negative Definite): $(-1)^k \Delta_k > 0$ for all $k$ → local **maximum**.
- [ ] **Indefinite Hessian** (some $\Delta_k < 0$, signs not alternating properly) → **saddle point**.
- [ ] **Convex function** ⇒ every stationary point is a *global* minimum.
- [ ] **First-order Taylor** approximates locally; **second-order Taylor** captures curvature.
- [ ] Always **verify the nature of stationary points** with second-order or graphical tests.
- [ ] For KTU, state **necessary AND sufficient conditions** separately to score full marks.

---

> **End of Module 1 Notes — Engineering Application of Optimization & Stationary Points**
> *Aligned with KTU 2024 Scheme | PEECT637 — Optimization Techniques*

topics.title: Functions of Single and Two Variables
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEECT637 - OPTIMIZATION TECHNIQUES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Functions of Single and Two Variables

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Aligned)

In the context of **Optimization Techniques**, a **function** is a mathematical mapping that establishes a deterministic correspondence between inputs (decision variables) and a single scalar output (objective). When a function depends on exactly one independent variable, it is termed a **Function of a Single Variable**, denoted $f: \mathbb{R} \to \mathbb{R}$. When it depends on two independent variables, it is termed a **Function of Two Variables**, denoted $f: \mathbb{R}^{2} \to \mathbb{R}$.

Mathematically, these are expressed as:
$$f: \mathbb{R} \to \mathbb{R}, \quad y = f(x)$$
$$f: \mathbb{R}^{2} \to \mathbb{R}, \quad z = f(x_{1}, x_{2})$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The optimization of single-variable functions forms the foundation for understanding gradient-based methods, line search procedures, and one-dimensional minimization algorithms (e.g., Golden Section, Fibonacci, Newton-Raphson). Two-variable functions are critical for understanding unconstrained multivariable optimization (Hessian analysis, saddle points, convexity).

## 1.2 Domain, Range, and Codomain

| Term | Single Variable $f(x)$ | Two Variables $f(x, y)$ |
|------|------------------------|--------------------------|
| **Domain** ($D_f$) | Set of all real $x$ for which $f(x)$ is defined | Subset of $\mathbb{R}^{2}$ for which $f(x,y)$ is defined |
| **Codomain** | $\mathbb{R}$ | $\mathbb{R}$ |
| **Range** ($R_f$) | Set of all real outputs $f(x)$ for $x \in D_f$ | Set of all real outputs $f(x,y)$ for $(x,y) \in D_f$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Boundary Concept for KTU:** A point $x^* \in D_f$ is called a **boundary point** if every neighbourhood of $x^*$ contains points both inside and outside $D_f$. The set of all boundary points forms the **boundary** $\partial D_f$. Interior points form $int(D_f)$. This distinction is essential when applying the **Weierstrass Theorem** to guarantee the existence of global minima/maxima.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!TIP]
> **Plain-English Analogy — The Thermostat Analogy:**
> Imagine your room has only **one knob** controlling temperature (single variable $f(x)$). Turning it left or right changes the room's temperature in a predictable way. The "best comfort" point is where the temperature is exactly what you want — this is a **one-dimensional optimization**.
>
> Now imagine a sophisticated climate system with **two knobs**: one for temperature and one for humidity (two variables $f(x, y)$). The "best comfort" is now a point on a flat 2D surface. Finding it requires looking at *both* directions simultaneously — this is a **two-dimensional optimization**, leading to concepts like gradient (a vector pointing uphill) and the Hessian (a matrix describing the local curvature of the terrain).

## 1.4 Types of Stationary Points (Geometric Intuition)

A stationary point $x^*$ satisfies $f'(x^*) = 0$ (single variable) or $\nabla f(x^*) = \mathbf{0}$ (two variables).

- **Local Minimum:** $f(x^*) \leq f(x)$ for all $x$ near $x^*$. *Analogy: the bottom of a valley.*
- **Local Maximum:** $f(x^*) \geq f(x)$ for all $x$ near $x^*$. *Analogy: the top of a hill.*
- **Saddle Point (only in $\geq 2$ variables):** Curvature is positive in some directions and negative in others. *Analogy: the pass between two mountains where you can descend either way depending on which direction you step.*

> [!WARNING]
> **Critical KTU Misconception:** Setting $f'(x) = 0$ yields a *necessary* but **NOT sufficient** condition for an extremum. A solution could be a saddle point or an inflection point. Always apply the **second derivative test** or the **Hessian test** before declaring a point a true minimum or maximum.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Functions of a Single Variable — Core Analytical Framework

### 2.1.1 Continuity

A function $f(x)$ is **continuous at** $x = c$ if three conditions hold:
1. $f(c)$ is defined
2. $\lim_{x \to c} f(x)$ exists
3. $\lim_{x \to c} f(x) = f(c)$

Equivalent $\epsilon$-$\delta$ form: for every $\epsilon > 0$, there exists $\delta > 0$ such that
$$\vert x - c \vert < \delta \implies \vert f(x) - f(c) \vert < \epsilon$$

### 2.1.2 Differentiability

$f$ is **differentiable at** $x = c$ if the following limit exists finitely:
$$f'(c) = \lim_{h \to 0} \frac{f(c+h) - f(c)}{h}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Hierarchy Theorem (MUST memorize for KTU):** *Differentiability $\Rightarrow$ Continuity*, but **Continuity does NOT imply Differentiability** (e.g., $f(x) = \vert x \vert$ is continuous at $0$ but not differentiable there).

### 2.1.3 Taylor Series Expansion (Single Variable)

For a function $f$ that is $n+1$ times differentiable at $x = x^*$:
$$f(x) = f(x^*) + f'(x^*)(x - x^*) + \frac{f''(x^*)}{2!}(x - x^*)^{2} + \dots + \frac{f^{(n)}(x^*)}{n!}(x - x^*)^{n} + R_{n+1}$$

The remainder term (Lagrange form):
$$R_{n+1} = \frac{f^{(n+1)}(\xi)}{(n+1)!}(x - x^*)^{n+1}, \quad \xi \in (x^*, x)$$

### 2.1.4 Necessary and Sufficient Conditions for Extrema

Let $f: \mathbb{R} \to \mathbb{R}$ be twice differentiable.

**Necessary Condition (First-Order):** If $x^*$ is a local extremum (and $f$ is differentiable), then
$$f'(x^*) = 0$$
Such a point is called a **critical point** or **stationary point**.

**Sufficient Condition (Second-Order):**
- If $f'(x^*) = 0$ and $f''(x^*) > 0$, then $x^*$ is a **strict local minimum**.
- If $f'(x^*) = 0$ and $f''(x^*) < 0$, then $x^*$ is a **strict local maximum**.
- If $f'(x^*) = 0$ and $f''(x^*) = 0$, the test is **inconclusive**; higher derivatives must be examined.

## 2.2 Functions of Two Variables — Core Analytical Framework

### 2.2.1 Partial Derivatives

The **first-order partial derivative** of $z = f(x, y)$ with respect to $x$ at $(x_0, y_0)$:
$$\frac{\partial f}{\partial x}\bigg|_{(x_0, y_0)} = f_{x}(x_0, y_0) = \lim_{h \to 0} \frac{f(x_0 + h, y_0) - f(x_0, y_0)}{h}$$

Similarly for $\dfrac{\partial f}{\partial y}$. Geometrically, $f_x$ is the slope of the tangent line along the $x$-direction (with $y$ held fixed).

### 2.2.2 Higher-Order Partial Derivatives

$$f_{xx} = \frac{\partial^{2} f}{\partial x^{2}}, \quad f_{yy} = \frac{\partial^{2} f}{\partial y^{2}}, \quad f_{xy} = \frac{\partial^{2} f}{\partial y \, \partial x}, \quad f_{yx} = \frac{\partial^{2} f}{\partial x \, \partial y}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Schwarz / Young's Theorem (Clairaut's Theorem):** If $f_{xy}$ and $f_{yx}$ are both **continuous** in a neighbourhood of $(x_0, y_0)$, then
> $$f_{xy}(x_0, y_0) = f_{yx}(x_0, y_0)$$
> This is a **HIGH-FREQUENCY KTU question** — always state the continuity assumption explicitly.

### 2.2.3 Gradient Vector

The **gradient** of $f: \mathbb{R}^{2} \to \mathbb{R}$ is the vector of first-order partials:
$$\nabla f(x, y) = \begin{bmatrix} \dfrac{\partial f}{\partial x} \\[2mm] \dfrac{\partial f}{\partial y} \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} f_x \\ f_y \end{bmatrix}$$

Geometrically, $\nabla f$ points in the direction of **steepest ascent** and is **perpendicular to the level curves** $f(x, y) = c$.

### 2.2.4 Hessian Matrix

The **Hessian** $H(x, y)$ collects all second-order partial derivatives:
$$H(x, y) = \nabla^{2} f(x, y) = \begin{bmatrix} f_{xx} & f_{xy} \\ f_{yx} & f_{yy} \end{bmatrix}$$

Under the continuity assumption of Schwarz's theorem, $H$ is **symmetric**.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **EXAMINATION GOLD:** This table consolidates every formula you must memorize for KTU Module 1.

| Concept | Single Variable Formula | Two Variables Formula | KTU Importance |
|---------|--------------------------|------------------------|----------------|
| **Function Form** | $y = f(x)$ | $z = f(x, y)$ | Foundation |
| **First Derivative** | $f'(x) = \lim_{h \to 0} \dfrac{f(x+h)-f(x)}{h}$ | $f_x, f_y$ (partials) | $\star \star \star$ |
| **Gradient** | Scalar $f'(x)$ | $\nabla f = [f_x, f_y]^{T}$ | $\star \star \star$ |
| **Second Derivative** | $f''(x)$ | Hessian $H = \begin{bmatrix} f_{xx} & f_{xy} \\ f_{yx} & f_{yy} \end{bmatrix}$ | $\star \star \star$ |
| **Necessary Condition** | $f'(x^*) = 0$ | $\nabla f(x^*, y^*) = \mathbf{0}$ | $\star \star \star$ |
| **Second-Order Test** | Sign of $f''(x^*)$ | Leading principal minors of $H$ | $\star \star \star$ |
| **Taylor Expansion** | $f(x^*+\Delta) = f(x^*) + f'(x^*)\Delta + \tfrac{1}{2}f''(x^*)\Delta^{2}$ | $f(\mathbf{x}^* + \Delta\mathbf{x}) = f(\mathbf{x}^*) + \nabla f^{T}\Delta\mathbf{x} + \tfrac{1}{2}\Delta\mathbf{x}^{T} H \Delta\mathbf{x}$ | $\star \star$ |
| **Convexity Test** | $f''(x) \geq 0 \;\; \forall x$ | $H$ positive semidefinite $\forall (x,y)$ | $\star \star$ |

### 2.3.1 Principal Minors of the Hessian (KTU High-Yield)

For $H = \begin{bmatrix} a & b \\ b & d \end{bmatrix}$ evaluated at a critical point $(x^*, y^*)$:

$$\Delta_{1} = a = f_{xx}(x^*, y^*)$$
$$\Delta_{2} = \det(H) = ad - b^{2} = f_{xx} f_{yy} - f_{xy}^{2}$$

**Classification Rule:**

- If $\Delta_{2} > 0$ and $\Delta_{1} > 0$ $\Rightarrow$ **Local Minimum** (Hessian positive definite)
- If $\Delta_{2} > 0$ and $\Delta_{1} < 0$ $\Rightarrow$ **Local Maximum** (Hessian negative definite)
- If $\Delta_{2} < 0$ $\Rightarrow$ **Saddle Point** (Hessian indefinite)
- If $\Delta_{2} = 0$ $\Rightarrow$ **Test Inconclusive** (higher-order or direct method needed)

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Application Domain | Use of Function Analysis |
|--------------------|--------------------------|
| **Mechanical Design** | Minimize stress, weight, or deflection over geometric variables |
| **Electrical Networks** | Minimize power loss as a function of resistance parameters |
| **Manufacturing** | Cost functions of two inputs (labour hours, material) optimized for minimum cost |
| **Economics / Operations Research** | Utility functions of consumption goods; profit maximization over price & quantity |
| **Machine Learning** | Loss functions of weights — gradient & Hessian drive training algorithms (Newton's method) |

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Derivation 1 — Single Variable Optimization (Taylor Series Justification of the Second-Order Test)

We want to rigorously show: *If $f'(x^*) = 0$ and $f''(x^*) > 0$, then $x^*$ is a strict local minimum.*

### Setup
Let $x^*$ be a stationary point, i.e., $f'(x^*) = 0$. Assume $f$ is twice continuously differentiable at $x^*$. Consider any $x$ near $x^*$, and set $\Delta x = x - x^*$.

### Step-by-Step Taylor Expansion

By Taylor's theorem with Lagrange remainder:
$$f(x) = f(x^*) + f'(x^*) \Delta x + \frac{f''(\xi)}{2!} (\Delta x)^{2}$$

where $\xi$ lies between $x$ and $x^*$.

### Substituting the Stationary Condition

Since $f'(x^*) = 0$, the first-order term vanishes:
$$f(x) = f(x^*) + \frac{f''(\xi)}{2} (\Delta x)^{2}$$

### Local Continuity Argument

Because $f''$ is continuous and $f''(x^*) > 0$, there exists a small neighbourhood $\mathcal{N}(x^*)$ such that for all $\xi \in \mathcal{N}(x^*)$:
$$f''(\xi) > \frac{f''(x^*)}{2} > 0$$

### Final Inequality

Hence, for any $x \in \mathcal{N}(x^*)$ with $x \neq x^*$:
$$f(x) - f(x^*) = \frac{f''(\xi)}{2}(\Delta x)^{2} > 0$$

Therefore, $f(x) > f(x^*)$ for all $x$ near (but not equal to) $x^*$, which is precisely the definition of a **strict local minimum**. $\blacksquare$

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Trick:** Memorize this Taylor expansion derivation — it is a favorite 7-mark Part B question. The key step is *cancelling the first-order term using $f'(x^*) = 0$*.

---

## 3.2 Worked Derivation 2 — Two-Variable Second-Order Sufficient Condition

### Statement to Prove

*Let $f: \mathbb{R}^{2} \to \mathbb{R}$ be twice continuously differentiable. Let $(x^*, y^*)$ satisfy $\nabla f(x^*, y^*) = \mathbf{0}$. If the Hessian $H(x^*, y^*)$ is **positive definite**, then $(x^*, y^*)$ is a strict local minimum.*

### Taylor Expansion in $\mathbb{R}^{2}$

For $\Delta \mathbf{x} = (h, k)^{T}$ with $(x, y) = (x^*, y^*) + (h, k)$:
$$f(x^*+h, y^*+k) = f(x^*, y^*) + \nabla f^{T} \begin{bmatrix} h \\ k \end{bmatrix} + \frac{1}{2} \begin{bmatrix} h & k \end{bmatrix} H(\xi) \begin{bmatrix} h \\ k \end{bmatrix}$$

where $\xi$ lies on the segment connecting $(x^*, y^*)$ and $(x^*+h, y^*+k)$.

### Cancellation of Linear Term

Since $\nabla f(x^*, y^*) = \mathbf{0}$:
$$f(x^*+h, y^*+k) - f(x^*, y^*) = \frac{1}{2} \begin{bmatrix} h & k \end{bmatrix} H(\xi) \begin{bmatrix} h \\ k \end{bmatrix}$$

### Sign Analysis

By continuity of the Hessian entries, for $(h, k)$ sufficiently small, $H(\xi)$ remains positive definite. For a positive definite matrix $H(\xi)$:
$$\begin{bmatrix} h & k \end{bmatrix} H(\xi) \begin{bmatrix} h & k \end{bmatrix} > 0 \quad \forall (h, k) \neq (0, 0)$$

### Conclusion

Therefore,
$$f(x^*+h, y^*+k) > f(x^*, y^*)$$
for all small $(h, k) \neq (0, 0)$, establishing $(x^*, y^*)$ as a **strict local minimum**. $\blacksquare$

---

## 3.3 Worked Derivation 3 — Quadratic Form Criterion for Hessian Positive Definiteness (2×2 case)

For a symmetric matrix $H = \begin{bmatrix} a & b \\ b & d \end{bmatrix}$ and vector $\mathbf{v} = \begin{bmatrix} v_1 \\ v_2 \end{bmatrix} \neq \mathbf{0}$:

### Expanding the Quadratic Form

$$\mathbf{v}^{T} H \mathbf{v} = \begin{bmatrix} v_1 & v_2 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} a & b \\ b & d \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} v_1 \\ v_2 \end{bmatrix}$$

Multiplying step-by-step:
$$= \begin{bmatrix} v_1 & v_2 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} a v_1 + b v_2 \\ b v_1 + d v_2 \end{bmatrix}$$

$$= a v_1^{2} + b v_1 v_2 + b v_1 v_2 + d v_2^{2}$$

$$= a v_1^{2} + 2b v_1 v_2 + d v_2^{2}$$

### Completing the Square (Sylvester's Criterion Justification)

We isolate the $v_1$ terms:
$$= a \left( v_1^{2} + \frac{2b}{a} v_1 v_2 \right) + d v_2^{2}$$

Completing the square:
$$= a \left( v_1 + \frac{b}{a} v_2 \right)^{2} - \frac{b^{2}}{a} v_2^{2} + d v_2^{2}$$

$$= a \left( v_1 + \frac{b}{a} v_2 \right)^{2} + \left( d - \frac{b^{2}}{a} \right) v_2^{2}$$

$$= a \left( v_1 + \frac{b}{a} v_2 \right)^{2} + \frac{ad - b^{2}}{a} v_2^{2}$$

### Sign Conditions for Positivity

For $\mathbf{v}^{T} H \mathbf{v} > 0$ for all $\mathbf{v} \neq \mathbf{0}$, both coefficients must be strictly positive (note: $v_2$ may be zero while $v_1 \neq 0$, and vice-versa):

$$a > 0 \quad \text{and} \quad \frac{ad - b^{2}}{a} > 0$$

Since $a > 0$, the second inequality simplifies to $ad - b^{2} > 0$.

### Sylvester's Conditions (Final Form)

$$\boxed{f_{xx} > 0 \quad \text{and} \quad f_{xx} f_{yy} - f_{xy}^{2} > 0 \iff H \text{ is positive definite}}$$

This confirms the KTU formula sheet rule: $\Delta_{1} > 0$ and $\Delta_{2} > 0$. $\blacksquare$

---

## 3.4 Comprehensive Worked Example (Full Solution)

**Problem:** Find and classify all stationary points of
$$f(x, y) = x^{3} - 3xy^{2} + 3x^{2} + 3y^{2}$$

### Step 1 — Compute First-Order Partials

$$f_x = \frac{\partial f}{\partial x} = 3x^{2} - 3y^{2} + 6x$$

$$f_y = \frac{\partial f}{\partial y} = -6xy + 6y = 6y(1 - x)$$

### Step 2 — Solve $\nabla f = \mathbf{0}$

From $f_y = 0$:
$$6y(1 - x) = 0 \implies y = 0 \quad \text{or} \quad x = 1$$

**Case 1: $y = 0$.** Substitute into $f_x = 0$:
$$3x^{2} + 6x = 0 \implies 3x(x + 2) = 0 \implies x = 0 \;\text{or}\; x = -2$$

Critical points: $(0, 0)$ and $(-2, 0)$.

**Case 2: $x = 1$.** Substitute into $f_x = 0$:
$$3(1)^{2} - 3y^{2} + 6(1) = 0 \implies 9 - 3y^{2} = 0 \implies y^{2} = 3 \implies y = \pm\sqrt{3}$$

Critical points: $(1, \sqrt{3})$ and $(1, -\sqrt{3})$.

### Step 3 — Compute Second-Order Partials (Hessian Entries)

$$f_{xx} = 6x + 6$$
$$f_{yy} = -6x + 6$$
$$f_{xy} = f_{yx} = -6y$$

### Step 4 — Evaluate Hessian and Principal Minors at Each Critical Point

**At $(0, 0)$:**
$$H = \begin{bmatrix} 6 & 0 \\ 0 & 6 \end{bmatrix}$$
$$\Delta_{1} = 6 > 0, \quad \Delta_{2} = 36 - 0 = 36 > 0$$
Since both positive $\Rightarrow$ **Local Minimum**.

**At $(-2, 0)$:**
$$H = \begin{bmatrix} -6 & 0 \\ 0 & 18 \end{bmatrix}$$
$$\Delta_{1} = -6 < 0, \quad \Delta_{2} = -108 - 0 = -108 < 0$$
Since $\Delta_{2} < 0 \Rightarrow$ **Saddle Point**.

**At $(1, \sqrt{3})$:**
$$H = \begin{bmatrix} 12 & -6\sqrt{3} \\ -6\sqrt{3} & 0 \end{bmatrix}$$
$$\Delta_{1} = 12 > 0, \quad \Delta_{2} = (12)(0) - (-6\sqrt{3})^{2} = -108 < 0$$
Since $\Delta_{2} < 0 \Rightarrow$ **Saddle Point**.

**At $(1, -\sqrt{3})$:**
$$H = \begin{bmatrix} 12 & 6\sqrt{3} \\ 6\sqrt{3} & 0 \end{bmatrix}$$
$$\Delta_{1} = 12 > 0, \quad \Delta_{2} = 0 - 108 = -108 < 0$$
Since $\Delta_{2} < 0 \Rightarrow$ **Saddle Point**.

### Step 5 — Final Classification Table

| Critical Point | $f_{xx}$ | $\Delta_2$ | Classification | Function Value |
|----------------|-----------|------------|----------------|----------------|
| $(0, 0)$ | $6$ | $36$ | **Local Minimum** | $0$ |
| $(-2, 0)$ | $-6$ | $-108$ | **Saddle Point** | $-8$ |
| $(1, \sqrt{3})$ | $12$ | $-108$ | **Saddle Point** | $1 + 3 + 9 = 13$ |
| $(1, -\sqrt{3})$ | $12$ | $-108$ | **Saddle Point** | $13$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineer's Insight:** The function $f(x, y) = x^{3} - 3xy^{2} + 3x^{2} + 3y^{2}$ behaves like a **monkey saddle** surface near the origin (cubical structure). The unique minimum at $(0,0)$ is a deep well, while the other three critical points are saddle configurations — interesting from a topological perspective in design optimization.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**State Clairaut's theorem (Schwarz's theorem) on the equality of mixed partial derivatives. What assumption is essential for its validity?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> Clairaut's theorem states that if $f: \mathbb{R}^{2} \to \mathbb{R}$ has continuous mixed second-order partial derivatives $f_{xy}$ and $f_{yx}$ at a point $(a, b)$, then:
> $$f_{xy}(a, b) = f_{yx}(a, b)$$
> **Essential Assumption:** Both mixed partial derivatives $f_{xy}$ and $f_{yx}$ must be **continuous** in a neighbourhood of $(a, b)$. Without continuity, equality is not guaranteed.

---

### Question 2 — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Define a stationary point of $f(x, y)$. Why is it called only a *necessary* and not a sufficient condition for an extremum?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> A point $(x^*, y^*)$ is a **stationary point** of $f(x, y)$ if
> $$\nabla f(x^*, y^*) = \begin{bmatrix} f_x(x^*, y^*) \\ f_y(x^*, y^*) \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 0 \end{bmatrix}$$
> It is necessary because any local extremum (interior point) must have zero gradient. It is **not sufficient** because stationary points include saddle points and inflection-type points where no extremum exists. The Hessian second-order test must be applied for sufficiency.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full-Length 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

### Question A — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model]`

**(a)** Find the critical points of the function
$$f(x, y) = x^{3} + y^{3} - 3xy$$
and classify each as a local minimum, local maximum, or saddle point using the Hessian test. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** State and prove the second-order sufficient condition for a local minimum of a function $f(x, y)$ assuming the Hessian is positive definite. Use the Taylor series expansion in $\mathbb{R}^{2}$ rigorously. **[7 Marks]**

#### Model Solution for (a) — 7 Marks

**Step 1: Compute first partial derivatives.**

$$f_x = 3x^{2} - 3y, \quad f_y = 3y^{2} - 3x$$

**Step 2: Solve $\nabla f = \mathbf{0}$.**

From $f_y = 0$: $3y^{2} = 3x \implies x = y^{2}$.

Substitute into $f_x = 0$:
$$3(y^{2})^{2} - 3y = 0 \implies 3y^{4} - 3y = 0 \implies 3y(y^{3} - 1) = 0$$

Hence $y = 0$ or $y^{3} = 1 \implies y = 1$.

Corresponding $x$ values:
- If $y = 0$: $x = 0$ → Critical point $(0, 0)$.
- If $y = 1$: $x = 1$ → Critical point $(1, 1)$.

**Step 3: Compute Hessian entries.**

$$f_{xx} = 6x, \quad f_{yy} = 6y, \quad f_{xy} = -3$$

**Step 4: Evaluate at each critical point.**

**At $(0, 0)$:**
$$H = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & -3 \\ -3 & 0 \end{bmatrix}, \quad \Delta_1 = 0, \quad \Delta_2 = 0 - 9 = -9 < 0$$
Since $\Delta_2 < 0$, $\Rightarrow$ **Saddle Point**.

**At $(1, 1)$:**
$$H = \begin{bmatrix} 6 & -3 \\ -3 & 6 \end{bmatrix}, \quad \Delta_1 = 6 > 0, \quad \Delta_2 = 36 - 9 = 27 > 0$$
Since $\Delta_1 > 0$ and $\Delta_2 > 0$, $\Rightarrow$ **Strict Local Minimum**.

**Step 5: Value at minimum.**
$$f(1, 1) = 1 + 1 - 3 = -1$$

#### Model Solution for (b) — 7 Marks

**Statement (2 Marks):** Let $f: \mathbb{R}^{2} \to \mathbb{R}$ be twice continuously differentiable at $(x^*, y^*)$. If
$$\nabla f(x^*, y^*) = \mathbf{0} \quad \text{and} \quad H(x^*, y^*) \text{ is positive definite,}$$
then $(x^*, y^*)$ is a **strict local minimum** of $f$.

**Proof (5 Marks):**

Let $\Delta \mathbf{x} = (h, k)^{T}$ and apply Taylor's theorem in two variables up to the second order. There exists $\xi$ on the segment joining $(x^*, y^*)$ and $(x^*+h, y^*+k)$ such that:

$$f(x^*+h, y^*+k) = f(x^*, y^*) + \nabla f(x^*, y^*)^{T} \begin{bmatrix} h \\ k \end{bmatrix} + \frac{1}{2} \begin{bmatrix} h & k \end{bmatrix} H(\xi) \begin{bmatrix} h \\ k \end{bmatrix}$$

Since $\nabla f(x^*, y^*) = \mathbf{0}$, the linear term vanishes:
$$f(x^*+h, y^*+k) - f(x^*, y^*) = \frac{1}{2} \begin{bmatrix} h & k \end{bmatrix} H(\xi) \begin{bmatrix} h \\ k \end{bmatrix}$$

Because the Hessian is continuous and positive definite at $(x^*, y^*)$, by continuity $H(\xi)$ is positive definite in a small neighbourhood. Therefore, for all $(h, k) \neq (0, 0)$ sufficiently small:

$$\begin{bmatrix} h & k \end{bmatrix} H(\xi) \begin{bmatrix} h & k \end{bmatrix} > 0$$

Hence,
$$f(x^*+h, y^*+k) > f(x^*, y^*)$$
proving $(x^*, y^*)$ is a **strict local minimum**. $\blacksquare$

---

### Question B — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model]` (Alternative Choice)

**(a)** Discuss the necessary and sufficient conditions for a function $f(x)$ of a single variable to have a local extremum at $x = x^*$. Apply your conditions to determine the extrema of
$$f(x) = x^{3} - 6x^{2} + 9x + 2$$ **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** State the Taylor series expansion of a function of two variables up to the second-order term. Using this expansion, derive the sufficient conditions (using principal minors) for a local minimum of $f(x, y)$. **[7 Marks]**

#### Model Solution for (a) — 7 Marks

**Theory (3 Marks):**

**Necessary Condition:** If $f$ is differentiable at $x^*$ and $x^*$ is a local extremum (interior point), then $f'(x^*) = 0$.

**Sufficient Conditions:**
- If $f'(x^*) = 0$ and $f''(x^*) > 0$ → Strict local minimum.
- If $f'(x^*) = 0$ and $f''(x^*) < 0$ → Strict local maximum.
- If $f'(x^*) = 0$ and $f''(x^*) = 0$ → Test inconclusive.

**Application (4 Marks):**

Given $f(x) = x^{3} - 6x^{2} + 9x + 2$.

First derivative:
$$f'(x) = 3x^{2} - 12x + 9 = 3(x^{2} - 4x + 3) = 3(x-1)(x-3)$$

Setting $f'(x) = 0$ gives critical points $x = 1$ and $x = 3$.

Second derivative:
$$f''(x) = 6x - 12$$

**At $x = 1$:** $f''(1) = 6 - 12 = -6 < 0$ → **Local Maximum**.
$$f(1) = 1 - 6 + 9 + 2 = 6$$

**At $x = 3$:** $f''(3) = 18 - 12 = 6 > 0$ → **Local Minimum**.
$$f(3) = 27 - 54 + 27 + 2 = 2$$

#### Model Solution for (b) — 7 Marks

**Taylor Series in Two Variables (2 Marks):**

For $f: \mathbb{R}^{2} \to \mathbb{R}$ that is twice continuously differentiable at $(x^*, y^*)$, with $\Delta x = h, \Delta y = k$:

$$f(x^*+h, y^*+k) = f(x^*, y^*) + f_x h + f_y k + \frac{1}{2}(f_{xx} h^{2} + 2 f_{xy} hk + f_{yy} k^{2}) + \text{higher order}$$

In compact notation:
$$f(\mathbf{x}^* + \Delta\mathbf{x}) = f(\mathbf{x}^*) + \nabla f^{T} \Delta\mathbf{x} + \frac{1}{2} \Delta\mathbf{x}^{T} H \Delta\mathbf{x}$$

**Derivation of Sufficient Conditions (5 Marks):**

At a critical point, $\nabla f = \mathbf{0}$, so:
$$f(\mathbf{x}^* + \Delta\mathbf{x}) - f(\mathbf{x}^*) = \frac{1}{2} \Delta\mathbf{x}^{T} H \Delta\mathbf{x}$$

For this to be **positive for all** $\Delta\mathbf{x} \neq \mathbf{0}$, $H$ must be **positive definite**. By Sylvester's criterion for a 2×2 symmetric matrix:

$$\Delta_1 = f_{xx} > 0 \quad \text{and} \quad \Delta_2 = f_{xx} f_{yy} - f_{xy}^{2} > 0$$

Equivalently, all **principal minors of $H$** must be positive. These two conditions together guarantee a **local minimum**. For a **local maximum**, $H$ must be negative definite:
$$\Delta_1 = f_{xx} < 0 \quad \text{and} \quad \Delta_2 > 0$$

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **🚨 Common Pitfalls That Cost Marks:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to verify $\nabla f = \mathbf{0}$:** Many students jump straight to the Hessian test without first confirming the critical point is indeed stationary. **Cost: 1–2 marks lost.**
>
> 2. **Ignoring the equality $f_{xy} = f_{yx}$:** When computing Hessian entries, students often write the mixed partial only once or use inconsistent notation. Always verify equality using Schwarz's theorem (under continuity).
>
> 3. **Misclassifying saddle points:** When $\Delta_2 < 0$, the point is a **saddle point**, NOT a maximum and NOT a minimum. Many students write "maximum" or "minimum" instead of "**saddle point**". This is an automatic **2-mark deduction**.
>
> 4. **Reporting the test inconclusive incorrectly:** When $\Delta_2 = 0$, you must write "**test inconclusive**" or use higher-order analysis. Do **not** guess "minimum" or "maximum".
>
> 5. **Single-variable pitfall:** When $f'(x^*) = 0$ and $f''(x^*) = 0$, do not stop — write "second derivative test inconclusive, requires higher-order analysis" to earn full marks.
>
> 6. **Units / Domain oversight:** If the question specifies a constrained domain, remember that global extrema may lie on the **boundary** — this requires Lagrange multipliers (Module 2/3) or boundary evaluation.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **🔥 Rapid Revision Checklist — Functions of Single and Two Variables**

**Single Variable Essentials:**
- ✅ $f'(x^*) = 0$ is the **necessary** condition for an interior extremum.
- ✅ $f''(x^*) > 0 \Rightarrow$ Minimum; $f''(x^*) < 0 \Rightarrow$ Maximum; $f''(x^*) = 0 \Rightarrow$ Inconclusive.
- ✅ Differentiability implies continuity, **not** vice-versa.
- ✅ Taylor expansion: $f(x) = f(x^*) + f'(x^*)\Delta x + \tfrac{1}{2}f''(\xi)\Delta x^{2}$.

**Two-Variable Essentials:**
- ✅ Gradient $\nabla f = [f_x, f_y]^T$ points in the direction of **steepest ascent**.
- ✅ Critical point condition: $\nabla f(x^*, y^*) = \mathbf{0}$.
- ✅ Hessian $H = \begin{bmatrix} f_{xx} & f_{xy} \\ f_{yx} & f_{yy} \end{bmatrix}$ — symmetric when partials are continuous.
- ✅ **Principal Minors Rule:**
  - $\Delta_1 > 0, \Delta_2 > 0$ → **Local Minimum** (H positive definite).
  - $\Delta_1 < 0, \Delta_2 > 0$ → **Local Maximum** (H negative definite).
  - $\Delta_2 < 0$ → **Saddle Point** (H indefinite).
  - $\Delta_2 = 0$ → **Inconclusive**.
- ✅ Schwarz / Clairaut's theorem requires continuity of mixed partials.
- ✅ Taylor expansion in $\mathbb{R}^{2}$: $f(\mathbf{x}^* + \Delta \mathbf{x}) = f(\mathbf{x}^*) + \nabla f^{T} \Delta \mathbf{x} + \tfrac{1}{2}\Delta \mathbf{x}^{T} H \Delta \mathbf{x}$.

**Problem-Solving Workflow:**
- ✅ Step 1: Compute $\nabla f$ and solve $\nabla f = \mathbf{0}$.
- ✅ Step 2: Compute Hessian entries $f_{xx}, f_{yy}, f_{xy}$.
- ✅ Step 3: Evaluate $\Delta_1, \Delta_2$ at each critical point.
- ✅ Step 4: Apply the principal minors rule.
- ✅ Step 5: State the classification explicitly with the function value.

**Memory Aid (Acronym: SHARP):**
- **S**tationary condition ($\nabla f = \mathbf{0}$)
- **H**essian construction
- **A**pply principal minors
- **R**ecognize saddle cases ($\Delta_2 < 0$)
- **P**resent value and classification

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Convexity and Concavity of Functions - Definition of Global and Local Optima - Optimality Criteria
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEECT637 - OPTIMIZATION TECHNIQUES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Convexity and Concavity of Functions - Definition of Global and Local Optima - Optimality Criteria

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Convex Functions — Formal KTU Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Convex Function)**
> A function $f: \mathbb{R} \to \mathbb{R}$ is said to be **convex** on an interval $I$ if for every pair of points $x_1, x_2 \in I$ and for every scalar $\lambda \in [0, 1]$:
> $$f(\lambda x_1 + (1-\lambda) x_2) \leq \lambda f(x_1) + (1-\lambda) f(x_2)$$

Geometrically, this means the **line segment joining any two points on the graph** of $f$ lies **above or on** the graph itself. In other words, the chord never dips below the curve.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Strictly Convex Function)**
> If the inequality is **strict** ($<$) for $x_1 \neq x_2$ and $\lambda \in (0,1)$, then $f$ is called **strictly convex**.

## 1.2 Concave Functions — Formal KTU Definition

A function $f$ is **concave** on $I$ if for every $x_1, x_2 \in I$ and $\lambda \in [0, 1]$:

$$f(\lambda x_1 + (1-\lambda) x_2) \geq \lambda f(x_1) + (1-\lambda) f(x_2)$$

The chord joining any two points on the graph lies **below or on** the curve.

> [!TIP]
> **Quick Memory Trick:** If $-f(x)$ is convex, then $f(x)$ is concave. This duality is heavily tested in KTU valuation scripts.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

**Real-World Analogy — The Valley and the Hill:**
- A **convex function** behaves like the inside of a **valley (U-shape)**. Imagine standing inside a smooth bowl. Every point on the rim is *higher* than any point inside. The lowest point (the bowl's bottom) is what optimization algorithms seek.
- A **concave function** behaves like the outside of a **hill (inverted U-shape)**. The chord connecting two points on the hill slopes above the surface, and the *peak* is the optimal point.
- Think of $f(x) = x^2$ (convex, valley) versus $f(x) = -x^2$ (concave, hill). Optimization of a convex function finds its **bottom (minimum)**, while optimization of a concave function finds its **top (maximum)**.

## 1.4 Global vs Local Optima — Definitions

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Local Minimum)**
> A point $x^* \in S$ is a **local minimum** of $f$ on the feasible set $S$ if there exists an $\varepsilon > 0$ such that:
> $$f(x^*) \leq f(x) \quad \forall x \in S \cap \{x : \vert x - x^* \vert < \varepsilon\}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Global Minimum)**
> A point $x^* \in S$ is a **global minimum** of $f$ on $S$ if:
> $$f(x^*) \leq f(x) \quad \forall x \in S$$

The **strict** versions replace $\leq$ with $<$. Similar definitions hold for local and global maxima with the inequality reversed.

## 1.5 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics in **Bold**

| Term | Symbol | Domain |
|---|---|---|
| **Gradient (slope)** | $f'(x)$ | First derivative |
| **Hessian (curvature)** | $f''(x)$ | Second derivative |
| **Neighborhood radius** | $\varepsilon$ | **Epsilon > 0** |
| **Convex combination weight** | $\lambda$ | $[0,1]$ |
| **Tolerance (KTU lab)** | $\delta$ | **Delta = 0.001** |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Why Convexity Matters in Optimization — The "Why"

The single most important theorem in KTU Module 1 is the **Convexity-Optimality Equivalence Theorem**:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Theorem 2.1 (Gold Standard for Board Exams)**
> If $f(x)$ is a **convex function** on a convex set $S$, then **every local minimum of $f$ on $S$ is also a global minimum**.

This is the reason convex programming dominates engineering design optimization: once you find a stationary point, you are guaranteed it is the best possible answer.

## 2.2 Operational Steps to Identify Convexity / Concavity

For a **single-variable function $f(x)$**:
1. Compute the **first derivative** $f'(x)$.
2. Compute the **second derivative** $f''(x)$.
3. Apply the **second-order convexity test**:
   - If $f''(x) \geq 0$ for all $x \in I$ ⟹ $f$ is **convex**.
   - If $f''(x) \leq 0$ for all $x \in I$ ⟹ $f$ is **concave**.
   - If $f''(x) > 0$ everywhere ⟹ **strictly convex**.
   - If $f''(x) < 0$ everywhere ⟹ **strictly concave**.

## 2.3 Optimality Criteria — Necessary and Sufficient Conditions

### 2.3.1 First-Order Necessary Condition (FONC)
If $x^*$ is a **local minimum** of $f$ and $f$ is differentiable at $x^*$, then:

$$f'(x^*) = 0$$

The gradient (slope) must vanish at any interior local optimum.

### 2.3.2 Second-Order Necessary Condition (SONC)
If $x^*$ is a local minimum, then:

$$f''(x^*) \geq 0$$

### 2.3.3 Second-Order Sufficient Condition (SOSC)
If $f'(x^*) = 0$ **and** $f''(x^*) > 0$, then $x^*$ is a **strict local minimum**.

If $f'(x^*) = 0$ **and** $f''(x^*) < 0$, then $x^*$ is a **strict local maximum**.

If $f'(x^*) = 0$ **and** $f''(x^*) = 0$, the test is **inconclusive** — higher-order derivatives must be examined.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Mathematical Statement | Condition |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Convexity definition | $f(\lambda x_1 + (1-\lambda) x_2) \leq \lambda f(x_1) + (1-\lambda) f(x_2)$ | $\lambda \in [0,1]$ |
| 2 | Concavity definition | $f(\lambda x_1 + (1-\lambda) x_2) \geq \lambda f(x_1) + (1-\lambda) f(x_2)$ | $\lambda \in [0,1]$ |
| 3 | 2nd-order convexity test | $f''(x) \geq 0 \ \forall x$ | Convex |
| 4 | 2nd-order concavity test | $f''(x) \leq 0 \ \forall x$ | Concave |
| 5 | FONC | $f'(x^*) = 0$ | Necessary for local min |
| 6 | SONC | $f''(x^*) \geq 0$ | Necessary for local min |
| 7 | SOSC (minimum) | $f'(x^*) = 0, \ f''(x^*) > 0$ | Sufficient for strict local min |
| 8 | SOSC (maximum) | $f'(x^*) = 0, \ f''(x^*) < 0$ | Sufficient for strict local max |
| 9 | Convex ⇔ local = global | If $f$ convex on convex $S$ | Every local min is global |
| 10 | Stationary point | $f'(x^*) = 0$ | May be min, max, or inflection |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Structural Design:** Convex optimization ensures minimum weight / cost truss designs have global optimality guarantees.
- **Machine Learning:** Loss functions (e.g., MSE) are designed convex so gradient descent converges to the global minimum.
- **Power Systems:** Economic dispatch problems are formulated as convex programs.
- **Signal Processing:** LASSO regression exploits convexity for sparse solutions.
- **Manufacturing:** Concave cost functions help in profit-maximization production planning.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation: Proof that $f(x) = x^2$ is Strictly Convex

We must show $f(\lambda x_1 + (1-\lambda) x_2) < \lambda f(x_1) + (1-\lambda) f(x_2)$ for $x_1 \neq x_2$ and $\lambda \in (0,1)$.

**Step 1:** Substitute $f(x) = x^2$ on the left-hand side.

$$\text{LHS} = [\lambda x_1 + (1-\lambda) x_2]^2$$

**Step 2:** Expand the square.

$$\text{LHS} = \lambda^2 x_1^2 + 2\lambda(1-\lambda)x_1 x_2 + (1-\lambda)^2 x_2^2$$

**Step 3:** Write the right-hand side.

$$\text{RHS} = \lambda x_1^2 + (1-\lambda) x_2^2$$

**Step 4:** Subtract LHS from RHS.

$$\text{RHS} - \text{LHS} = \lambda x_1^2 + (1-\lambda) x_2^2 - \lambda^2 x_1^2 - 2\lambda(1-\lambda)x_1 x_2 - (1-\lambda)^2 x_2^2$$

**Step 5:** Group the $x_1^2$ terms.

$$\lambda x_1^2 - \lambda^2 x_1^2 = \lambda(1-\lambda) x_1^2$$

**Step 6:** Group the $x_2^2$ terms.

$$(1-\lambda) x_2^2 - (1-\lambda)^2 x_2^2 = \lambda(1-\lambda) x_2^2$$

**Step 7:** Combine everything.

$$\text{RHS} - \text{LHS} = \lambda(1-\lambda)[x_1^2 + x_2^2 - 2 x_1 x_2]$$

**Step 8:** Recognize the perfect square.

$$x_1^2 + x_2^2 - 2 x_1 x_2 = (x_1 - x_2)^2$$

**Step 9:** Final result.

$$\text{RHS} - \text{LHS} = \lambda(1-\lambda)(x_1 - x_2)^2$$

**Step 10:** Conclusion.

Since $\lambda \in (0,1)$, both $\lambda > 0$ and $(1-\lambda) > 0$. Also $(x_1 - x_2)^2 > 0$ when $x_1 \neq x_2$. Therefore:

$$\text{RHS} - \text{LHS} > 0 \implies f(x) = x^2 \text{ is strictly convex.} \quad \blacksquare$$

## 3.2 Worked Example: Apply Optimality Criteria to $f(x) = x^3 - 3x$

**Step 1:** Compute the first derivative.

$$f'(x) = 3x^2 - 3$$

**Step 2:** Apply FONC by setting $f'(x^*) = 0$.

$$3x^2 - 3 = 0 \implies x^2 = 1 \implies x^* = \pm 1$$

**Step 3:** Compute the second derivative.

$$f''(x) = 6x$$

**Step 4:** Apply SOSC at each critical point.

At $x^* = -1$:

$$f''(-1) = 6(-1) = -6 < 0$$

Therefore, $x^* = -1$ is a **strict local maximum**.

At $x^* = +1$:

$$f''(1) = 6(1) = 6 > 0$$

Therefore, $x^* = +1$ is a **strict local minimum**.

**Step 5:** Check for global extrema on $\mathbb{R}$.

As $x \to +\infty$, $f(x) \to +\infty$. As $x \to -\infty$, $f(x) \to -\infty$. Hence neither local extremum is global on the entire real line.

**Step 6:** If the domain is restricted to $[-2, 2]$, evaluate at endpoints.

$$f(-2) = -8 + 6 = -2, \quad f(2) = 8 - 6 = 2$$

Global minimum on $[-2, 2]$ is $-2$ at $x = -2$ (endpoint). Global maximum is $2$ at $x = 2$ (endpoint).

## 3.3 Exhaustive Example: Convexity Test for $f(x) = e^x$

**Step 1:** First derivative.

$$f'(x) = e^x$$

**Step 2:** Second derivative.

$$f''(x) = e^x$$

**Step 3:** Evaluate sign of $f''(x)$ over $\mathbb{R}$.

$$e^x > 0 \quad \forall x \in \mathbb{R}$$

**Step 4:** Conclusion. Since $f''(x) > 0$ everywhere, $f(x) = e^x$ is **strictly convex** on $\mathbb{R}$.

**Step 5:** Consequence for optimization. Every local minimum of $e^x$ is also the global minimum. Since $e^x > 0$ for all $x$ with $e^x \to 0$ as $x \to -\infty$, the infimum is $0$ (not attained) — there is no global minimum on $\mathbb{R}$, but every stationary point (none, since $e^x \neq 0$) would be global.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram: Classification of Critical Points

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA["Start: f differentiable at x*"] --> nodeB{"Is f prime x* = 0?"}
    nodeB -- "No" --> nodeC["x* is NOT a critical point"]
    nodeB -- "Yes" --> nodeD["x* is a Stationary Point"]
    nodeD --> nodeE{"Evaluate f double prime x*"}
    nodeE -- "f double prime x* > 0" --> nodeF["Strict Local MINIMUM"]
    nodeE -- "f double prime x* < 0" --> nodeG["Strict Local MAXIMUM"]
    nodeE -- "f double prime x* = 0" --> nodeH["INCONCLUSIVE: Higher Order Test Needed"]
    nodeH --> nodeI{"Examine f triple prime x*"}
    nodeI -- "Triple prime non-zero" --> nodeJ["Point of Inflection Saddle"]
    nodeI -- "Triple prime equals zero" --> nodeK["Continue to f 4th derivative"]
    nodeF --> nodeL{"Is f convex on S?"}
    nodeG --> nodeL
    nodeL -- "Yes" --> nodeM["Local = Global Optimum"]
    nodeL -- "No" --> nodeN["May NOT be Global"]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram: Convex vs Concave Function Comparison

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA["Function f of x"] --> nodeB{"Sign of f double prime x"}
    nodeB -- "Always POSITIVE" --> nodeC["CONVEX: U-Shape Valley"]
    nodeB -- "Always NEGATIVE" --> nodeD["CONCAVE: Inverted U-Shape Hill"]
    nodeB -- "Changes SIGN" --> nodeE["NEITHER: Saddle Shape"]
    nodeC --> nodeF["Global MIN lies at stationary point"]
    nodeD --> nodeG["Global MAX lies at stationary point"]
    nodeE --> nodeH["Multiple local extrema possible"]
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Schematic: Optimality Criteria Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA["Given unconstrained problem: Minimize f of x"] --> nodeB["Step 1: Compute f prime x"]
    nodeB --> nodeC["Step 2: Solve f prime x = 0"]
    nodeC --> nodeD["Step 3: Get candidate points x sub 1 x sub 2 ... x sub n"]
    nodeD --> nodeE["Step 4: Compute f double prime x sub i"]
    nodeE --> nodeF{"f double prime x sub i"}
    nodeF -- "> 0" --> nodeG["Local Minimum at x sub i"]
    nodeF -- "< 0" --> nodeH["Local Maximum at x sub i"]
    nodeF -- "= 0" --> nodeI["Higher-Order Analysis Required"]
    nodeG --> nodeJ{"Check Convexity of f on Domain"}
    nodeH --> nodeJ
    nodeI --> nodeJ
    nodeJ -- "f is convex" --> nodeK["Local Min = Global Min"]
    nodeJ -- "f is concave" --> nodeL["Local Max = Global Max"]
    nodeJ -- "Neither" --> nodeM["Compare all candidate f values"]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## PART A QUESTIONS (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam - May 2024]
**Define a convex function. State the second-order condition for a twice-differentiable function to be convex.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

A function $f: \mathbb{R} \to \mathbb{R}$ is convex on interval $I$ if for all $x_1, x_2 \in I$ and $\lambda \in [0,1]$:

$$f(\lambda x_1 + (1-\lambda) x_2) \leq \lambda f(x_1) + (1-\lambda) f(x_2)$$

**Second-order condition:** If $f$ is twice differentiable, then $f$ is convex on $I$ if and only if $f''(x) \geq 0$ for all $x \in I$.

**Distribution (KTU Valuation):** Definition = 2 marks, Second-order condition = 1 mark.

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]
**Distinguish between local minimum and global minimum with a suitable example.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

- **Local Minimum:** $x^*$ is a local minimum if $\exists \varepsilon > 0$ such that $f(x^*) \leq f(x)$ for all $x$ in $\varepsilon$-neighborhood of $x^*$.
- **Global Minimum:** $x^*$ is a global minimum if $f(x^*) \leq f(x)$ for **all** $x$ in the feasible set $S$.

**Example:** $f(x) = x^4 - 4x^2 + 1$. Critical points at $x = \pm\sqrt{2}, 0$. Local maxima at $x = 0$ and local minima at $x = \pm\sqrt{2}$. The **global** minimum is at both $\pm\sqrt{2}$ since $f(\pm\sqrt{2}) = -3 < f(0) = 1$.

**Distribution:** Two definitions = 2 marks, Example = 1 mark.

---

## PART B QUESTIONS (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### QUESTION A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - Dec 2024 Model]

**(a) [7 Marks]** State and prove the necessary and sufficient conditions for a local minimum of a twice-differentiable function $f(x)$ at $x = x^*$.

**(b) [7 Marks]** For $f(x) = 2x^3 - 9x^2 + 12x + 3$, find all critical points, classify them using second-order conditions, and identify the global minimum on $[0, 3]$.

#### Model Solution:

**(a) Necessary and Sufficient Conditions for Local Minimum:**

**Necessary Condition (FONC + SONC):** If $f$ is differentiable at $x^*$ and $x^*$ is a local minimum, then:

$$f'(x^*) = 0 \quad \text{and} \quad f''(x^*) \geq 0$$

**Sufficient Condition (SOSC):** If $f'(x^*) = 0$ and $f''(x^*) > 0$, then $x^*$ is a strict local minimum.

**Proof of Sufficiency (using Taylor expansion):**

For $f$ twice differentiable near $x^*$, by Taylor's theorem:
