> 1. **Forgetting the max{} term in penalty functions:** Students often write $P = f + \mu g^2$ even when $g < 0$, which is wrong — the penalty should only apply to *violations*.
> 2. **Sign errors in Lagrangian:** The Lagrangian is $f + \lambda^T h$, NOT $f - \lambda^T h$. Markers deduct 1–2 marks immediately.
> 3. **Not verifying second-order sufficiency:** Many students stop after finding the stationary point. For full marks, always check $\nabla^2 L \succ 0$ on the tangent space.
> 4. **Confusing interior vs exterior:** Barrier = interior (feasible), Penalty = exterior (infeasible OK). Mixing them up costs 2–3 marks.
> 5. **Forgetting to update $\lambda$ in ALM:** Students often treat ALM as a pure penalty, missing the multiplier update step — losing 2 marks.
> 6. **Wrong inequality for barrier domain:** Strict inequality $g_i(x) < 0$ is required for $\ln(-g_i)$ to be defined. Students often write $\le$.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE] **High-Density Revision Checklist (Print Before Exam)**

- ✅ **Constrained Optimization:** $\min f(x)$ s.t. $g_i(x)\le 0$, $h_j(x)=0$.
- ✅ **Lagrangian:** $L = f(x) + \lambda^T h(x) + \mu^T g(x)$.
- ✅ **FONC (Equality):** $\nabla_x L = 0$ AND $h(x) = 0$.
- ✅ **Quadratic Penalty:** $P(x,\mu_k) = f + \mu_k\sum[\max\{0,g_i\}]^2$, $\mu_k \uparrow$.
- ✅ **Log Barrier:** $B(x,r_k) = f - r_k\sum\ln(-g_i)$, $r_k \downarrow$.
- ✅ **Inverse Barrier:** $B_{inv} = f + r_k\sum 1/(-g_i)$.
- ✅ **Augmented Lagrangian:** $\mathcal{L}_A = f + \lambda^T h + \frac{\mu}{2}\|h\|^2$.
- ✅ **Multiplier Update:** $\lambda^{k+1} = \lambda^k + \mu h(x_k)$.
- ✅ **Penalty grows by factor** $c \in [2,10]$ per iteration.
- ✅ **Barrier requires strictly feasible starting point.**
- ✅ **ALM avoids ill-conditioning** by keeping $\mu$ bounded.
- ✅ **Second-order sufficiency:** $\nabla^2 L \succ 0$ on tangent space of active constraints.
- ✅ **KKT Complementary Slackness:** $\lambda_i^* g_i(x^*) = 0$, $\lambda_i^* \ge 0$ for inequality problems.
- ✅ **Engineering applications:** Structural design (stress), trajectory (path), economic dispatch (capacity), SVM (margin).
- ✅ **KTU favourite questions:** "Apply ALM to…" (7 marks), "Solve by Lagrange multipliers" (7 marks), "Compare Penalty vs Barrier" (7 marks).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Lagrangian Function - Inequality Constrained Techniques - KKT Conditions - Constrained Steepest Descent Method
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEECT637 - OPTIMIZATION TECHNIQUES
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Lagrangian Function - Inequality Constrained Techniques - KKT Conditions - Constrained Steepest Descent Method

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

### 1.1 The Constrained Optimization Landscape

In engineering design problems, we rarely have the freedom to optimize a system without boundaries. Whether it is minimizing the weight of a structural beam subject to a maximum stress constraint, or minimizing the cost of a chemical reactor subject to safety constraints, **real engineering optimization is inherently constrained**. The mathematical machinery developed to handle such problems — the **Lagrangian function** and the **Karush-Kuhn-Tucker (KKT) conditions** — represents the crown jewel of classical nonlinear programming.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU PEECT637 - Module 3):**
> The KKT conditions generalize the classical method of Lagrange multipliers (which handles only equality constraints) to problems with **inequality constraints**. They provide **necessary conditions** for a local optimum in smooth nonlinear programs and, under convexity assumptions, become **sufficient conditions** for a global optimum.

### 1.2 Formal Mathematical Definition

Consider the general nonlinear programming (NLP) problem in standard form:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Minimize } \quad & f(\mathbf{x}) \\
\text{subject to } \quad & g_i(\mathbf{x}) \leq 0, \quad i = 1, 2, \ldots, m \\
& h_j(\mathbf{x}) = 0, \quad j = 1, 2, \ldots, p
\end{aligned}
$$

where $\mathbf{x} = (x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_n)^T \in \mathbb{R}^n$, $f: \mathbb{R}^n \rightarrow \mathbb{R}$, and $g_i, h_j: \mathbb{R}^n \rightarrow \mathbb{R}$ are continuously differentiable functions.

**The Lagrangian Function** $L: \mathbb{R}^n \times \mathbb{R}^m \times \mathbb{R}^p \rightarrow \mathbb{R}$ is defined as:

$$
L(\mathbf{x}, \boldsymbol{\lambda}, \boldsymbol{\mu}) = f(\mathbf{x}) + \sum_{i=1}^{m} \lambda_i \, g_i(\mathbf{x}) + \sum_{j=1}^{p} \mu_j \, h_j(\mathbf{x})
$$

The vectors $\boldsymbol{\lambda} = (\lambda_1, \ldots, \lambda_m)^T$ and $\boldsymbol{\mu} = (\mu_1, \ldots, \mu_p)^T$ are called the **Lagrange multipliers** (or **dual variables** or **KKT multipliers**). Each $\lambda_i$ is associated with the inequality constraint $g_i(\mathbf{x}) \leq 0$.

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The Mountain Climber Inside a Fenced Boundary

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy: The Climber in a Circular Fence**
>
> Imagine a blindfolded mountaineer standing inside a circular fenced enclosure on a hilly terrain, trying to reach the lowest valley. The terrain function is $f(\mathbf{x})$ and the fence represents the inequality constraint $g(\mathbf{x}) \leq 0$.
>
> - If the **valley lies strictly inside the fence**, the climber behaves like an unconstrained problem — the fence does not push back. The corresponding multiplier $\lambda = 0$ (analogous to a rope with zero tension).
> - If the **valley lies exactly at the fence boundary**, the rope (constraint) is taut and exerts a non-zero force $\lambda > 0$, preventing the climber from leaving the boundary.
> - If the **valley lies strictly outside the fence**, then no feasible optimum exists at that location — the climber can never reach it.
>
> The **complementary slackness condition** $\lambda \cdot g(\mathbf{x}) = 0$ captures this beautifully: either the constraint is *active* (taut rope, $\lambda > 0$) and binds the optimum, or it is *inactive* (slack rope, $\lambda = 0$) and irrelevant to the optimum. **Both cannot be positive simultaneously.**

### 1.4 Why KKT Conditions Matter in Engineering

The KKT conditions unify all stationary-point characterizations of constrained optimization under a single elegant framework:

| Classical Concept | KKT Equivalent |
| :--- | :--- |
| Unconstrained stationary point | $\nabla f = 0$ with $\lambda = 0$ |
| Equality-constrained optimum (Classical Lagrange) | $\nabla f + \mu \nabla h = 0$ |
| Inequality boundary optimum | $\nabla f + \lambda \nabla g = 0$, $\lambda \geq 0$ |
| Interior optimum | $\nabla f = 0$, $\lambda = 0$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Relevance:** The KKT conditions appear in nearly every KTU examination paper for this course — both as theoretical statements (Part A) and as computational tools (Part B). Mastering them is non-negotiable.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

### 2.1 The Four Pillars of KKT Conditions

For the NLP problem defined in Section 1.2, a feasible point $\mathbf{x}^*$ is a **local minimum** if there exist vectors $\boldsymbol{\lambda}^*$ and $\boldsymbol{\mu}^*$ such that the following four conditions hold simultaneously:

#### 2.1.1 Stationarity Condition (Gradient Condition)

$$
\nabla_{\mathbf{x}} L(\mathbf{x}^*, \boldsymbol{\lambda}^*, \boldsymbol{\mu}^*) = \mathbf{0}
$$

In expanded form:

$$
\nabla f(\mathbf{x}^*) + \sum_{i=1}^{m} \lambda_i^* \, \nabla g_i(\mathbf{x}^*) + \sum_{j=1}^{p} \mu_j^* \, \nabla h_j(\mathbf{x}^*) = \mathbf{0}
$$

**Physical meaning:** The negative gradient of the objective $-\nabla f$ must be expressible as a non-negative linear combination of the gradients of the active inequality constraints plus an arbitrary linear combination of the gradients of the equality constraints. In other words, the descent direction must lie in the cone spanned by the outward normals of the active constraints.

#### 2.1.2 Primal Feasibility

$$
g_i(\mathbf{x}^*) \leq 0, \quad i = 1, 2, \ldots, m
$$

$$
h_j(\mathbf{x}^*) = 0, \quad j = 1, 2, \ldots, p
$$

**Meaning:** The candidate optimum must satisfy all original constraints.

#### 2.1.3 Dual Feasibility (Non-negativity of Inequality Multipliers)

$$
\lambda_i^* \geq 0, \quad i = 1, 2, \ldots, m
$$

**Meaning:** Multipliers for inequality constraints cannot be negative. This reflects the convention that the feasible region lies on the *negative* side of the gradient $\nabla g_i$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Sign Convention Alert:** The KKT conditions require the constraints to be written in the form $g_i(\mathbf{x}) \leq 0$. If your constraint is given as $x + y \geq 4$, you MUST convert it to $4 - x - y \leq 0$ (i.e., $g(\mathbf{x}) = 4 - x - y \leq 0$) before applying KKT. **This is a common source of sign errors in KTU examinations.**

#### 2.1.4 Complementary Slackness

$$
\lambda_i^* \, g_i(\mathbf{x}^*) = 0, \quad i = 1, 2, \ldots, m
$$

**Meaning:** For each inequality constraint, **at least one** of the following must be zero:

- The constraint is **active**: $g_i(\mathbf{x}^*) = 0$ (the boundary is binding), allowing $\lambda_i^*$ to be positive.
- The multiplier is **zero**: $\lambda_i^* = 0$ (the constraint is irrelevant), allowing $g_i(\mathbf{x}^*) < 0$.

It is impossible for both $\lambda_i^*$ and $-g_i(\mathbf{x}^*)$ to be positive simultaneously.

### 2.2 Second-Order Sufficient Conditions

If $\mathbf{x}^*$ satisfies the KKT necessary conditions and additionally the **Hessian of the Lagrangian** projected onto the tangent space of active constraints is positive definite, then $\mathbf{x}^*$ is a **strict local minimum**:

$$
\mathbf{d}^T \, \nabla_{\mathbf{x}\mathbf{x}}^2 L(\mathbf{x}^*, \boldsymbol{\lambda}^*, \boldsymbol{\mu}^*) \, \mathbf{d} > 0, \quad \forall \mathbf{d} \neq \mathbf{0}
$$

such that $\nabla g_i(\mathbf{x}^*)^T \mathbf{d} = 0$ for all active inequalities and $\nabla h_j(\mathbf{x}^*)^T \mathbf{d} = 0$ for all equalities.

### 2.3 Constraint Qualifications (CQ)

KKT conditions are only **necessary** when a suitable **Constraint Qualification** holds at the optimum. The most commonly used CQ is:

**LICQ (Linear Independence Constraint Qualification):** The gradients $\nabla g_i(\mathbf{x}^*)$ for all active inequalities and $\nabla h_j(\mathbf{x}^*)$ for all equalities must be linearly independent.

Other CQs include MFCQ (Mangasarian-Fromovitz), Slater's condition (for convex problems), and Abadie CQ.

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Concept | Mathematical Expression | Interpretation |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Standard NLP form | $\min f(\mathbf{x})$ s.t. $g_i \leq 0$, $h_j = 0$ | Always write $\leq$ form before KKT |
| Lagrangian | $L = f + \sum \lambda_i g_i + \sum \mu_j h_j$ | Augmented objective function |
| Stationarity | $\nabla f + \sum \lambda_i \nabla g_i + \sum \mu_j \nabla h_j = 0$ | First-order balance |
| Primal Feasibility | $g_i(\mathbf{x}^*) \leq 0$, $h_j(\mathbf{x}^*) = 0$ | Original constraints |
| Dual Feasibility | $\lambda_i \geq 0$ | Sign of inequality multipliers |
| Complementary Slackness | $\lambda_i \, g_i(\mathbf{x}^*) = 0$ | Either $\lambda_i = 0$ or $g_i = 0$ |
| Active Set | $A(\mathbf{x}^*) = \{i \mid g_i(\mathbf{x}^*) = 0\}$ | Binding constraints |
| Second-Order Condition | $\mathbf{d}^T \nabla_{\mathbf{x}\mathbf{x}}^2 L \, \mathbf{d} > 0$ on tangent cone | Sufficiency |
| LICQ | $\nabla g_i, \nabla h_j$ linearly independent | Validity of KKT |
| Steepest Descent Direction | $\mathbf{d}_k = -\nabla f(\mathbf{x}_k)$ | Unconstrained |
| Projected Gradient | $\mathbf{d}_k = P_{\mathcal{F}}(-\nabla f(\mathbf{x}_k))$ | Feasible direction |
| Step Length Update | $\mathbf{x}_{k+1} = \mathbf{x}_k + \alpha_k \mathbf{d}_k$ | Line search result |

### 2.5 Engineering Applications of KKT

- **Structural Optimization:** Minimum weight truss design with stress and displacement constraints.
- **Power Systems:** Economic dispatch with generator capacity and emission constraints.
- **Process Engineering:** Optimal reactor design subject to thermodynamic feasibility.
- **Machine Learning:** Support Vector Machines (SVMs) are formulated and solved using KKT — every support vector corresponds to $\lambda_i > 0$.
- **Portfolio Optimization:** Markowitz mean-variance model with budget and risk constraints.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

### 3.1 Derivation: From Lagrangian to KKT Stationarity

Starting with the Lagrangian:

$$
L(\mathbf{x}, \boldsymbol{\lambda}, \boldsymbol{\mu}) = f(\mathbf{x}) + \sum_{i=1}^{m} \lambda_i \, g_i(\mathbf{x}) + \sum_{j=1}^{p} \mu_j \, h_j(\mathbf{x})
$$

Setting the partial derivatives with respect to $\mathbf{x}$ to zero (necessary condition for an extremum of $L$ with respect to $\mathbf{x}$):

$$
\frac{\partial L}{\partial x_k} = \frac{\partial f}{\partial x_k} + \sum_{i=1}^{m} \lambda_i \frac{\partial g_i}{\partial x_k} + \sum_{j=1}^{p} \mu_j \frac{\partial h_j}{\partial x_k} = 0, \quad k = 1, \ldots, n
$$

In vector form, this becomes the **stationarity condition**:

$$
\nabla f(\mathbf{x}) + \sum_{i=1}^{m} \lambda_i \nabla g_i(\mathbf{x}) + \sum_{j=1}^{p} \mu_j \nabla h_j(\mathbf{x}) = \mathbf{0}
$$

### 3.2 Worked Example: Complete KKT Solution

**Problem:** Minimize $f(x, y) = x^2 + y^2 - 4x - 4y$ subject to:

$$
g_1(x, y) : x + 2y - 4 \leq 0
$$

$$
g_2(x, y) : -x \leq 0 \quad (x \geq 0)
$$

$$
g_3(x, y) : -y \leq 0 \quad (y \geq 0)
$$

**Step 1: Construct the Lagrangian**

$$
L = x^2 + y^2 - 4x - 4y + \lambda_1(x + 2y - 4) + \lambda_2(-x) + \lambda_3(-y)
$$

**Step 2: Apply Stationarity**

$$
\frac{\partial L}{\partial x} = 2x - 4 + \lambda_1 - \lambda_2 = 0
$$

$$
\frac{\partial L}{\partial y} = 2y - 4 + 2\lambda_1 - \lambda_3 = 0
$$

**Step 3: Apply Complementary Slackness**

$$
\lambda_1 (x + 2y - 4) = 0
$$

$$
\lambda_2 (-x) = 0 \implies \lambda_2 x = 0
$$

$$
\lambda_3 (-y) = 0 \implies \lambda_3 y = 0
$$

**Step 4: Apply Dual Feasibility**

$$
\lambda_1 \geq 0, \quad \lambda_2 \geq 0, \quad \lambda_3 \geq 0
$$

**Step 5: Apply Primal Feasibility**

$$
x + 2y \leq 4, \quad x \geq 0, \quad y \geq 0
$$

**Step 6: Enumerate Cases via Complementary Slackness**

We test the $2^3 = 8$ possible combinations of active/inactive constraints. We focus on the most promising cases.

#### Case A: All constraints inactive ($\lambda_1 = \lambda_2 = \lambda_3 = 0$)

From stationarity: $2x - 4 = 0 \Rightarrow x = 2$ and $2y - 4 = 0 \Rightarrow y = 2$.

Check primal feasibility: $x + 2y = 2 + 4 = 6 \not\leq 4$. **INFEASIBLE.** Reject.

#### Case B: $g_1$ active only ($x + 2y = 4$, $\lambda_1 \geq 0$, $\lambda_2 = \lambda_3 = 0$)

Stationarity gives:

$$
2x - 4 + \lambda_1 = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \lambda_1 = 4 - 2x
$$

$$
2y - 4 + 2\lambda_1 = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad 2y - 4 + 2(4 - 2x) = 0
$$

Expanding the second equation:

$$
2y - 4 + 8 - 4x = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad 2y - 4x + 4 = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad y = 2x - 2
$$

Substituting into the active constraint $x + 2y = 4$:

$$
x + 2(2x - 2) = 4 \quad \Rightarrow \quad x + 4x - 4 = 4 \quad \Rightarrow \quad 5x = 8 \quad \Rightarrow \quad x = 1.6
$$

Therefore:

$$
y = 2(1.6) - 2 = 1.2
$$

Verify dual feasibility:

$$
\lambda_1 = 4 - 2(1.6) = 0.8 \geq 0 \quad \checkmark
$$

Verify primal feasibility:

$$
x = 1.6 \geq 0 \quad \checkmark, \quad y = 1.2 \geq 0 \quad \checkmark
$$

Verify complementary slackness: $\lambda_2 x = 0 \cdot 1.6 = 0$ ✓, $\lambda_3 y = 0 \cdot 1.2 = 0$ ✓.

**Optimal solution:** $\mathbf{x}^* = (1.6, \, 1.2)$, $\lambda_1^* = 0.8$, $\lambda_2^* = \lambda_3^* = 0$.

Optimal objective value:

$$
f^* = (1.6)^2 + (1.2)^2 - 4(1.6) - 4(1.2) = 2.56 + 1.44 - 6.4 - 4.8 = -7.2
$$

### 3.3 The Constrained Steepest Descent Method (Algorithm)

The **Constrained Steepest Descent Method** is the direct generalization of the unconstrained steepest descent algorithm to feasible-region-respecting iterates.

**Algorithm: Constrained Steepest Descent**

**Input:** Starting point $\mathbf{x}_0 \in \mathcal{F}$ (feasible), convergence tolerance $\epsilon > 0$, maximum iterations $N$.

**Step 1:** Initialize $k \leftarrow 0$.

**Step 2:** Compute the gradient $\nabla f(\mathbf{x}_k)$.

**Step 3:** Determine the **feasible descent direction** $\mathbf{d}_k$:

- If $-\nabla f(\mathbf{x}_k)$ points into the feasible region $\mathcal{F}$, set $\mathbf{d}_k = -\nabla f(\mathbf{x}_k)$.
- Otherwise, **project** the gradient onto the feasible region. The standard projected gradient direction is:

$$
\mathbf{d}_k = \mathbf{x}_k - P_{\mathcal{F}}\big(\mathbf{x}_k - \nabla f(\mathbf{x}_k)\big)
$$

where $P_{\mathcal{F}}(\mathbf{y})$ denotes the Euclidean projection of $\mathbf{y}$ onto $\mathcal{F}$.

Equivalently, by solving the auxiliary minimization:

$$
\mathbf{d}_k = \arg\min_{\mathbf{d}} \; \nabla f(\mathbf{x}_k)^T \mathbf{d} + \tfrac{1}{2}\Vert \mathbf{d} \Vert^2 \quad \text{s.t.} \; \mathbf{x}_k + \mathbf{d} \in \mathcal{F}
$$

**Step 4:** **Line search:** Find the optimal step length $\alpha_k$ that minimizes $f(\mathbf{x}_k + \alpha \mathbf{d}_k)$ subject to $\mathbf{x}_k + \alpha \mathbf{d}_k \in \mathcal{F}$:

$$
\alpha_k = \arg\min_{\alpha \geq 0} \; f(\mathbf{x}_k + \alpha \mathbf{d}_k)
$$

For quadratic objectives, the closed-form formula is:

$$
\alpha_k = -\frac{\nabla f(\mathbf{x}_k)^T \mathbf{d}_k}{\mathbf{d}_k^T \mathbf{Q} \, \mathbf{d}_k}
$$

where $\mathbf{Q} = \nabla^2 f$.

**Step 5:** Update: $\mathbf{x}_{k+1} = \mathbf{x}_k + \alpha_k \mathbf{d}_k$.

**Step 6:** Convergence check:

- If $\Vert \mathbf{d}_k \Vert < \epsilon$, **stop**. The projected gradient is zero, indicating a KKT point.
- Else, set $k \leftarrow k + 1$ and go to Step 2.

### 3.4 Worked Example: Constrained Steepest Descent

**Problem:** Minimize $f(x, y) = (x - 2)^2 + (y - 1)^2$ subject to $x + y \leq 2$ and $x \geq 0$, $y \geq 0$.

**Iteration 0:** Start at $\mathbf{x}_0 = (0, 0)^T$.

Gradient: $\nabla f(\mathbf{x}_0) = (-4, -2)^T$.

Check feasibility: $0 + 0 = 0 \leq 2$ ✓. Feasible.

Check direction feasibility: $-\nabla f = (4, 2)^T$. Moving in this direction increases $x + y$, eventually violating $x + y \leq 2$. So unconstrained steepest descent is not directly feasible.

Projection approach: Find $\mathbf{d}_0$ by solving

$$
\min_{\mathbf{d}} \; (-4)d_1 + (-2)d_2 + \tfrac{1}{2}(d_1^2 + d_2^2) \quad \text{s.t.} \quad d_1 + d_2 \leq 2, \, d_1 \geq -0, \, d_2 \geq 0
$$

The active constraint at $\mathbf{x}_0 = (0,0)$ is $x \geq 0$ and $y \geq 0$ (the lower bounds). The KKT sub-problem yields:

$$
\mathbf{d}_0 = \arg\min_{\mathbf{d}} \; \nabla f^T \mathbf{d} + \tfrac{1}{2}\Vert \mathbf{d}\Vert^2 \quad \text{s.t. constraints}
$$

The solution is $\mathbf{d}_0 = (4, 2)^T$ projected so that $\mathbf{x}_0 + \mathbf{d}_0 \in \mathcal{F}$. Since $\mathbf{x}_0 = (0,0)$ and the feasible region requires $x \geq 0, y \geq 0$, any $\mathbf{d}_0$ with positive components stays feasible up to $x + y \leq 2$.

Maximizing along direction $(4, 2)$: intersection with $x + y = 2$ occurs at $4\alpha + 2\alpha = 2$, so $\alpha = 1/3$.

So $\mathbf{d}_0 = (4/3, 2/3)^T$, and step length $\alpha_0 = 1/3$ to remain on boundary.

**Iteration 1:** $\mathbf{x}_1 = (0,0) + (1/3)(4, 2) = (4/3, 2/3)^T$.

Gradient: $\nabla f(\mathbf{x}_1) = (2 \cdot 4/3 - 4, \, 2 \cdot 2/3 - 2) = (-4/3, -2/3)^T$.

Now $-\nabla f = (4/3, 2/3)^T$, which points away from the feasible interior (since $4/3 + 2/3 = 2$, we are exactly on the boundary $x + y = 2$). So $\mathbf{d}_1 = (0, 0)^T$, indicating convergence.

**Result:** $\mathbf{x}^* = (4/3, 2/3)^T$, $f^* = (4/3 - 2)^2 + (2/3 - 1)^2 = ( -2/3)^2 + (-1/3)^2 = 4/9 + 1/9 = 5/9$.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

### 4.1 Mermaid Flowchart: KKT Algorithm Decision Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: NLP Problem Minimize f of x] --> B[Convert all constraints to g_i of x leq 0 form]
    B --> C[Construct Lagrangian L = f + sum lambda_i g_i + sum mu_j h_j]
    C --> D[Compute partial derivatives of L with respect to x]
    D --> E[Set stationarity condition gradient L equals 0]
    E --> F[Enumerate active constraint sets via complementary slackness]
    F --> G{Test candidate: primal feasibility}
    G -- Infeasible --> H[Discard candidate]
    G -- Feasible --> I[Test dual feasibility lambda_i geq 0]
    I -- Violated --> H
    I -- Satisfied --> J[Valid KKT candidate found]
    J --> K[Verify second-order sufficient conditions]
    K -- Positive definite on tangent cone --> L[OPTIMAL POINT x star]
    K -- Not satisfied --> M[May be saddle or maximum]
    H --> N{More cases to test}
    N -- Yes --> F
    N -- No --> O[No KKT solution in this branch]
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram: Constrained Steepest Descent Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph INIT[Initialization Block]
        init1[Set iteration counter k = 0]
        init2[Choose feasible starting point x0 in F]
        init3[Set tolerance epsilon]
    end
    INIT --> grad[Gradient Computation Module]
    grad --> dir[Direction Computation Module]
    dir --> proj[Projection onto Feasible Region F]
    proj --> ls[Line Search Module alpha k]
    ls --> upd[Update x k plus 1 equals x k plus alpha k d k]
    upd --> chk{Convergence Check d k norm less than epsilon}
    chk -- Not converged --> grad
    chk -- Converged --> term[Terminate Return x star]
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Diagram: Complementary Slackness Logic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S0[Constraint g_i of x star] --> Q1{Is g_i of x star equal to 0?}
    Q1 -- Yes Active --> P1[Constraint is binding at boundary]
    P1 --> R1{Is lambda_i strictly greater than 0?}
    R1 -- Yes --> OK1[KKT Valid: Active with positive multiplier]
    R1 -- No --> BAD1[Invalid: Active constraint requires lambda i geq 0]
    Q1 -- No Inactive --> P2[Constraint has slack]
    P2 --> R2{Is lambda_i equal to 0?}
    R2 -- Yes --> OK2[KKT Valid: Inactive with zero multiplier]
    R2 -- No --> BAD2[Invalid: Inactive constraint requires lambda i = 0]
```

### 4.4 Mermaid Block Diagram: KKT Conditions Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    L[Lagrangian Function L of x lambda mu] --> S[Stationarity: Gradient of L w.r.t. x equals 0]
    L --> D[Dual Feasibility: lambda i geq 0]
    L --> CS[Complementary Slackness: lambda i times g_i of x equals 0]
    PF[Primal Feasibility: g_i leq 0 h_j equals 0] --> KKT[K K T Point]
    S --> KKT
    D --> KKT
    CS --> KKT
    PF --> KKT
    CQ[Constraint Qualification: LICQ or MFCQ] --> KKT
    KKT --> SOSC{Second Order Sufficient Condition}
    SOSC -- Positive definite on tangent cone --> OPT[Local Minimum x star]
    SOSC -- Indeterminate --> UNK[Further Analysis Required]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

### Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Question 1: [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
*State the Karush-Kuhn-Tucker (KKT) necessary conditions for the following nonlinear programming problem:*

*Minimize $f(\mathbf{x})$ subject to $g_i(\mathbf{x}) \leq 0$, $i = 1, \ldots, m$ and $h_j(\mathbf{x}) = 0$, $j = 1, \ldots, p$.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The KKT necessary conditions state that if $\mathbf{x}^*$ is a local minimum and a suitable constraint qualification (e.g., LICQ) holds at $\mathbf{x}^*$, then there exist vectors $\boldsymbol{\lambda}^* \in \mathbb{R}^m$ and $\boldsymbol{\mu}^* \in \mathbb{R}^p$ such that:

**(1) Stationarity (1 Mark):**

$$
\nabla f(\mathbf{x}^*) + \sum_{i=1}^{m} \lambda_i^* \, \nabla g_i(\mathbf{x}^*) + \sum_{j=1}^{p} \mu_j^* \, \nabla h_j(\mathbf{x}^*) = \mathbf{0}
$$

**(2) Primal Feasibility (1 Mark):**

$$
g_i(\mathbf{x}^*) \leq 0, \, i = 1, \ldots, m; \quad h_j(\mathbf{x}^*) = 0, \, j = 1, \ldots, p
$$

**(3) Dual Feasibility (0.5 Marks):**

$$
\lambda_i^* \geq 0, \, i = 1, \ldots, m
$$

**(4) Complementary Slackness (0.5 Marks):**

$$
\lambda_i^* \, g_i(\mathbf{x}^*) = 0, \, i = 1, \ldots, m
$$

---

**Question 2: [KTU University Exam - May 2024]**
*Explain the complementary slackness condition in KKT conditions with a suitable example.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The complementary slackness condition states that $\lambda_i^* \, g_i(\mathbf{x}^*) = 0$ for each inequality constraint $g_i(\mathbf{x}) \leq 0$. This means that for every inequality constraint, **at most one** of the following is strictly positive: the multiplier $\lambda_i^*$ or the negative slack $-g_i(\mathbf{x}^*)$ (1 Mark).

**Interpretation (1 Mark):**

- If the constraint is **active** ($g_i(\mathbf{x}^*) = 0$, boundary binding), then the corresponding multiplier $\lambda_i^* \geq 0$ may be positive.
- If the constraint is **inactive** ($g_i(\mathbf{x}^*) < 0$, slack), then the multiplier must be zero ($\lambda_i^* = 0$).
- It is impossible for both the constraint to be strictly satisfied and the multiplier to be positive.

**Example (1 Mark):** For a problem $\min f(x)$ subject to $x \leq 5$, if the optimum occurs at $x^* = 5$ (active), then $\lambda^* > 0$. If the optimum occurs at $x^* = 3$ (interior, inactive), then $\lambda^* = 0$. In both cases $\lambda^* (x^* - 5) = 0$.

---

### Part B Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model Paper]**

**(a)** *Formulate the Lagrangian function and state the KKT conditions for the problem:* (7 Marks)

$$
\text{Minimize } f(x_1, x_2) = (x_1 - 1)^2 + (x_2 - 2)^2
$$

$$
\text{Subject to: } g_1 : x_1 + x_2 - 4 \leq 0, \quad g_2 : x_1 - 1 \geq 0, \quad g_3 : x_2 - 1 \geq 0
$$

**(b)** *Solve the above problem using the KKT conditions and find the optimal solution.* (7 Marks)

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Lagrangian Formulation (7 Marks):**

First, convert all inequalities to $g_i \leq 0$ form (2 Marks):

$$
g_1(x_1, x_2) = x_1 + x_2 - 4 \leq 0
$$

$$
g_2(x_1, x_2) = 1 - x_1 \leq 0 \quad \text{(rewriting } x_1 \geq 1 \text{ as } -x_1 \leq -1 \text{ i.e., } 1 - x_1 \leq 0\text{)}
$$

$$
g_3(x_1, x_2) = 1 - x_2 \leq 0
$$

Construct the Lagrangian (2 Marks):

$$
L = (x_1 - 1)^2 + (x_2 - 2)^2 + \lambda_1(x_1 + x_2 - 4) + \lambda_2(1 - x_1) + \lambda_3(1 - x_2)
$$

KKT Conditions (3 Marks):

Stationarity:

$$
\frac{\partial L}{\partial x_1} = 2(x_1 - 1) + \lambda_1 - \lambda_2 = 0
$$

$$
\frac{\partial L}{\partial x_2} = 2(x_2 - 2) + \lambda_1 - \lambda_3 = 0
$$

Primal Feasibility:

$$
x_1 + x_2 \leq 4, \quad x_1 \geq 1, \quad x_2 \geq 1
$$

Dual Feasibility: $\lambda_1, \lambda_2, \lambda_3 \geq 0$.

Complementary Slackness:

$$
\lambda_1(x_1 + x_2 - 4) = 0, \quad \lambda_2(1 - x_1) = 0, \quad \lambda_3(1 - x_2) = 0
$$

**(b) Solution (7 Marks):**

Test Case: All constraints inactive, $\lambda_1 = \lambda_2 = \lambda_3 = 0$.

From stationarity: $x_1 = 1, x_2 = 2$. Check $x_1 \geq 1$ ✓, $x_2 \geq 1$ ✓, $x_1 + x_2 = 3 \leq 4$ ✓. **Feasible and all KKT satisfied.**

Optimal solution: $\mathbf{x}^* = (1, 2)$, $f^* = 0$.

---

### Part B Question B (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model Paper]**

**(a)** *Explain the Lagrangian function for constrained optimization problems with inequality and equality constraints. Discuss the role of Lagrange multipliers.* (7 Marks)

**(b)** *Apply the Constrained Steepest Descent Method to solve:* (7 Marks)

$$
\min f(x_1, x_2) = x_1^2 + x_2^2 - 4x_1 - 4x_2
$$

subject to $x_1 + x_2 \leq 4$, $x_1 \geq 0$, $x_2 \geq 0$, starting from $\mathbf{x}_0 = (0, 0)^T$.

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Lagrangian Function Explanation (7 Marks):**

The Lagrangian function (3 Marks) combines the objective function with the constraints through multiplier weights:

$$
L(\mathbf{x}, \boldsymbol{\lambda}, \boldsymbol{\mu}) = f(\mathbf{x}) + \sum_{i=1}^{m} \lambda_i g_i(\mathbf{x}) + \sum_{j=1}^{p} \mu_j h_j(\mathbf{x})
$$

Role of Lagrange multipliers (4 Marks):

- **Economic interpretation:** $\lambda_i$ represents the rate of change of the optimal objective value per unit change in the $i$-th constraint's RHS (shadow price).
- **Geometric interpretation:** At the optimum, the negative gradient $-\nabla f$ is a conic combination of the gradients of active constraints.
- **Sensitivity analysis:** $\lambda_i > 0$ implies the constraint is active and binding; $\lambda_i = 0$ implies it is inactive.
- **Duality:** Each multiplier is a dual variable associated with a primal constraint, forming the foundation of Lagrangian duality theory.

**(b) Constrained Steepest Descent (7 Marks):**

$\mathbf{x}_0 = (0, 0)^T$, $\nabla f = (2x_1 - 4, 2x_2 - 4)^T$, so $\nabla f(\mathbf{x}_0) = (-4, -4)^T$.

Direction: $-\nabla f(\mathbf{x}_0) = (4, 4)^T$. Feasibility check: starting at $(0,0)$, the active constraints are $x_1 \geq 0$ and $x_2 \geq 0$.

Move along $(4, 4)^T$ until either $x_1 + x_2 = 4$ or until optimum on this direction.

Parametric line: $\mathbf{x}(\alpha) = (4\alpha, 4\alpha)$. Line search:

$$
\min_{\alpha} f(4\alpha, 4\alpha) = (4\alpha)^2 + (4\alpha)^2 - 4(4\alpha) - 4(4\alpha) = 32\alpha^2 - 32\alpha
$$

Derivative: $64\alpha - 32 = 0 \Rightarrow \alpha = 0.5$. So unconstrained optimum on this line is at $\alpha = 0.5$, giving $(2, 2)$. Check feasibility: $2 + 2 = 4 \leq 4$ ✓.

**Iteration 1:** $\mathbf{x}_1 = (2, 2)^T$. Gradient: $\nabla f(\mathbf{x}_1) = (0, 0)^T$.

Since gradient is zero, the KKT conditions are satisfied. **Convergence achieved.**

Optimal: $\mathbf{x}^* = (2, 2)^T$, $f^* = 4 + 4 - 8 - 8 = -8$.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
>
> 1. **Sign Convention Errors (–2 to –3 Marks):** Students frequently write constraints in the form $x + y \geq c$ instead of $c - x - y \leq 0$. This flips the sign of $\lambda$ and produces an incorrect "negative multiplier" result. **Always standardize before applying KKT.**
>
> 2. **Forgetting Complementary Slackness (–2 Marks):** Many students solve stationarity + primal + dual feasibility but skip checking $\lambda_i g_i = 0$. The KTU examiner deducts marks explicitly.
>
> 3. **Not Verifying All Case Enumerations (–1 to –2 Marks):** When multiple constraints are present, students often pick only one case (e.g., all active) without checking if simpler interior solutions exist.
>
> 4. **Confusing Sufficiency and Necessity (–1 Mark):** KKT conditions are **necessary** under CQ; they are **sufficient** only when second-order conditions are met. Stating "KKT implies optimum" without qualification is imprecise.
>
> 5. **In Steepest Descent, Forgetting Feasibility Projection (–2 Marks):** The descent direction must keep iterates within $\mathcal{F}$. Blindly using $\mathbf{d}_k = -\nabla f$ without projection is a standard error.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU Module 3 (Lagrangian, KKT, Constrained Steepest Descent)**
>
> ✅ **Lagrangian:** $L = f + \sum \lambda_i g_i + \sum \mu_j h_j$. Standardize ALL inequalities to $\leq 0$ form first.
>
> ✅ **Four KKT Conditions (in order):**
> - Stationarity: $\nabla f + \sum \lambda_i \nabla g_i + \sum \mu_j \nabla h_j = 0$
> - Primal Feasibility: $g_i \leq 0$, $h_j = 0$
> - Dual Feasibility: $\lambda_i \geq 0$ (inequalities only)
> - Complementary Slackness: $\lambda_i g_i = 0$
>
> ✅ **Constraint Qualification:** LICQ (linearly independent gradients at active constraints) must hold for KKT to be necessary.
>
> ✅ **Case Analysis Strategy:** Use complementary slackness to enumerate $(active, inactive)$ subsets; test each case.
>
> ✅ **Constraint is ACTIVE $\Rightarrow g_i(\mathbf{x}^*) = 0$, and $\lambda_i$ may be $> 0$.**
>
> ✅ **Constraint is INACTIVE $\Rightarrow g_i(\mathbf{x}^*) < 0$, and $\lambda_i$ MUST equal 0.**
>
> ✅ **Constrained Steepest Descent Algorithm:**
> - Start feasible $\mathbf{x}_0 \in \mathcal{F}$.
> - Compute $\nabla f(\mathbf{x}_k)$; project $-\nabla f$ onto feasible direction cone.
> - Line search for $\alpha_k$ maintaining feasibility.
> - Update $\mathbf{x}_{k+1} = \mathbf{x}_k + \alpha_k \mathbf{d}_k$.
> - Terminate when projected gradient norm < $\epsilon$.
>
> ✅ **Quadratic Line Search Formula:** $\alpha_k = -\frac{\nabla f^T \mathbf{d}_k}{\mathbf{d}_k^T \mathbf{Q} \, \mathbf{d}_k}$.
>
> ✅ **Engineering Context:** KKT conditions are foundational to SVMs, economic dispatch, structural optimization, and convex programming duality.
>
> ✅ **Common Exam Question Types:** (i) State KKT conditions, (ii) Solve via KKT with 2-3 variables, (iii) Apply steepest descent with constraint, (iv) Interpret multipliers economically.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Genetic Algorithms
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEECT637 - OPTIMIZATION TECHNIQUES > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Genetic Algorithms ## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Genetic Algorithm (GA):** A Genetic Algorithm is a **metaheuristic**, population-based stochastic search and optimization technique inspired by the principles of **natural selection** and **biological evolution** (Darwinian evolution). It belongs to the larger class of **Evolutionary Algorithms (EAs)** and operates on a population of candidate solutions (called *individuals* or *chromosomes*), iteratively applying biologically inspired operators — **Selection**, **Crossover (Recombination)**, and **Mutation** — to evolve progressively better solutions to a given optimization problem. In the KTU 2024 Scheme context, a GA is used to find the **global optimum** of complex, non-differentiable, discontinuous, or noisy objective functions where classical gradient-based methods (like Newton's method or Lagrange multipliers) fail or get trapped in local optima. ### 1.2 Key Terminology Mapping (Biology → GA) | Biological Term | GA Equivalent | Meaning | |---|---|---| | Chromosome | Encoded solution string | A candidate solution | | Gene | A single variable/parameter bit | One decision variable | | Allele | Value of a gene (0 or 1) | Possible value at a position | | Population | Set of candidate solutions | Search space sample | | Fitness | Objective function value | Quality measure | | Selection | Reproduction preference | Probabilistic survival | | Crossover | Recombination of parents | Exchange of genetic material | | Mutation | Random alteration | Maintains diversity | ### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Survival of the Fittest" Intuition > [!NOTE] > **Real-World Analogy — Imagine a Population of Competing Robots:** > > Imagine you have **100 robots** tasked with finding the deepest valley in a vast, mountainous, foggy terrain. They cannot see the bottom directly. Each robot has a random initial position. You **measure how deep each robot currently is** (this is the *fitness*). > > 1. The **deepest robots** (highest fitness) are given the highest probability to **reproduce** and pass on their position tendencies. > 2. They **combine** their coordinates (crossover) — like sharing two halves of a map. > 3. Occasionally, a robot **randomly shifts** its position (mutation) — to explore new terrain. > 4. Over many **generations**, the swarm of robots converges towards the deepest valley. > > **The robots = chromosomes, the terrain = search space, the depth = fitness, the generations = iterations.** This is exactly how a GA explores a solution space! ### 1.4 Standard Notation Used in KTU Problems > [!IMPORTANT] > **Standard GA Constants and Symbols:** > - **N** = Population size (commonly **50 to 200**) > - **L** = Chromosome length (number of bits/genes) > - **p_c** = Crossover probability (commonly **0.6 to 0.95**) > - **p_m** = Mutation probability (commonly **0.001 to 0.05**) > - **t** = Current generation number > - **T** = Maximum number of generations > - **f(x_i)** = Fitness of individual *i* > - **F̄** = Average population fitness > - **F_max** = Maximum fitness in current population ### 1.5 Why Use Genetic Algorithms? — Engineering Motivation GA is preferred when the objective function has one or more of the following characteristics: - **Non-differentiable** or discontinuous gradient information is unavailable. - The search space is **highly multimodal** with many local optima. - The objective involves **noisy simulation** data (e.g., CFD, FEA outputs). - The problem involves **combinatorial/discrete** decisions (e.g., TSP, scheduling). - The objective is a **black-box** function with expensive evaluations. **Engineering Applications:** Antenna design, VLSI circuit layout, structural optimization, job-shop scheduling, route planning, parameter tuning of PID controllers, machine learning hyperparameter optimization. ## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 The Five Canonical Phases of a Genetic Algorithm A standard GA operates through the following structured iterative cycle: #### **Phase 1 — Initialization** A population of *N* chromosomes is generated randomly (or using a heuristic seeding). Each chromosome is encoded as a binary string of length *L* representing one candidate solution. $$ x_i = (b_{i1}, b_{i2}, b_{i3}, \dots, b_{iL}), \quad b_{ij} \in \{0, 1\} $$ For continuous problems, **real-valued encoding** is also used: $$ x_i = (x_{i1}, x_{i2}, \dots, x_{in}), \quad x_{ij} \in [L_j, U_j] $$ where $L_j$ and $U_j$ are the lower and upper bounds of variable *j*. #### **Phase 2 — Fitness Evaluation** Each chromosome is decoded into the problem domain, and the objective function is evaluated. For a **maximization** problem, fitness is directly the objective value: $$ \text{fitness}(x_i) = f(x_i) $$ For **minimization**, a common transformation (e.g., ranking or inversion) is used: $$ \text{fitness}(x_i) = \frac{1}{1 + f(x_i) + \vert f_{\max} \vert} $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Scaling Caution:** KTU examiners often test whether students handle the sign and shift correctly when converting a minimization problem to a maximization-style fitness. Always check the sign and add a constant to avoid negative fitness values in roulette-wheel selection. #### **Phase 3 — Selection** Parents are probabilistically chosen with preference to higher-fitness individuals. Common selection methods are: | Selection Method | Mechanism | Selection Pressure | |---|---|---| | Roulette Wheel (Proportional) | Probability ∝ fitness | Medium | | Rank-Based | Probability ∝ rank | Tunable | | Tournament | Pick *k*, select best | High & tunable | | Truncation | Select top *T%* | Very high | | Stochastic Universal Sampling (SUS) | Multi-pointer roulette | Low variance | For **Roulette Wheel**, the probability of selecting individual *i* is: $$ P_i = \frac{f_i}{\sum_{j=1}^{N} f_j} $$ #### **Phase 4 — Crossover (Recombination)** With probability **p_c**, two parent chromosomes exchange segments to produce two offspring. Without crossover, offspring are exact copies of parents. Common crossover types: - **Single-point crossover** — A cut point *k* is chosen; bits after *k* are swapped. - **Two-point crossover** — Two cut points; middle segment is swapped. - **Uniform crossover** — Each bit independently chosen from one of the two parents with probability 0.5. - **Arithmetic crossover (real-coded)** — Weighted average of parents. Example (single-point at k=4): $$ \text{Parent 1: } 1011 \vert 01 \quad \text{Parent 2: } 0010 \vert 11 $$ $$ \text{Offspring 1: } 1011 \, 11 \quad \text{Offspring 2: } 0010 \, 01 $$ #### **Phase 5 — Mutation** With probability **p_m**, each bit is independently flipped (0 ↔ 1). Mutation preserves **genetic diversity** and prevents premature convergence. $$ b_{ij}' = \begin{cases} 1 - b_{ij}, & \text{if } U(0,1) < p_m \\ b_{ij}, & \text{otherwise} \end{cases} $$ For real-coded GAs, **Gaussian mutation** is common: $$ x_{ij}' = x_{ij} + \mathcal{N}(0, \sigma^2) $$ ### 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Scheme — Genetic Algorithms Master Formula Table** | # | Formula / Concept | Symbolic Expression | Purpose / Use | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | Binary Decoding (unsigned) | $x = L_b + \dfrac{\text{decimal}(b)}{2^L - 1} (U_b - L_b)$ | Convert binary → real value | | 2 | Binary Decoding (signed) | $x = -L_b + \dfrac{\text{decimal}(b)}{2^{L-1} - 1} (U_b - L_b)$ | For negative ranges | | 3 | Selection Probability | $P_i = \dfrac{f_i}{\sum_{j=1}^{N} f_j}$ | Roulette wheel selection | | 4 | Expected Count | $E_i = N \cdot P_i = \dfrac{N \cdot f_i}{\bar{f}}$ | Number of copies expected | | 5 | Average Fitness | $\bar{f} = \dfrac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} f_i$ | Population mean fitness | | 6 | Schema Theorem (Holland) | $E[m(H, t+1)] \geq m(H,t) \cdot \dfrac{f(H)}{\bar{f}} \cdot \left[1 - p_c \cdot \dfrac{\delta(H)}{L-1} - p_m \cdot o(H)\right]$ | Schema growth lower bound | | 7 | Schema Order | $o(H)$ | Number of fixed positions | | 8 | Defining Length | $\delta(H) = \text{last}(H) - \text{first}(H)$ | Distance between outer fixed bits | | 9 | Convergence Criterion | $\vert f_{\max} - \bar{f} \vert < \epsilon$ or $t = T$ | Termination check | | 10 | Mutation Rate Recommended | $p_m \approx \dfrac{1}{L}$ | Rule-of-thumb heuristic | | 11 | Crossover Rate Recommended | $p_c \in [0.6, 0.95]$ | Empirical range | | 12 | Population Size Heuristic | $N \in [50, 200]$ | Typical engineering practice | > [!NOTE] > **Engineering Utility:** GA is extensively used in **aerodynamic shape optimization** (wing profile design), where CFD evaluations are black-box. Each chromosome encodes parameters like camber, thickness ratio, and angle of attack. GA navigates this high-dimensional, noisy space better than gradient methods. ### 2.3 Holland's Schema Theorem — The Theoretical Engine of GA A **schema** is a similarity template where bits can be either fixed (0 or 1) or "don't care" (denoted *). The schema theorem states that short, low-order, above-average fitness schemata grow exponentially across generations. This is the **Building Block Hypothesis**: > Short, high-performing building blocks combine and propagate exponentially to form optimal solutions. ### 2.4 Termination Criteria The GA loop terminates when **any one** of the following conditions is met: 1. **Maximum generations** *T* reached. 2. **Convergence** — $\vert f_{\max} - \bar{f} \vert < \epsilon$ (population has stagnated). 3. **Best fitness target** achieved — $f_{\max} \geq f^*$. 4. **Time/computational budget** exhausted. 5. **No improvement** over *k* consecutive generations. ## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation: Roulette Wheel Selection Probability **Given:** A population of *N* individuals with fitness values $f_1, f_2, \dots, f_N$. **Goal:** Derive the probability that individual *i* is selected as a parent. **Step 1:** Define total fitness of the population. $$ F_{\text{total}} = \sum_{j=1}^{N} f_j $$ **Step 2:** Since the roulette wheel is divided into *N* slices proportional to each fitness, the probability of selecting individual *i* equals the ratio of its slice area to the total wheel area. $$ P_i = \frac{\text{Slice area of } i}{\text{Total wheel area}} $$ **Step 3:** Because slice area ∝ fitness, substitute proportionality. $$ P_i = \frac{f_i}{F_{\text{total}}} = \frac{f_i}{\sum_{j=1}^{N} f_j} $$ **Step 4:** Since probabilities must sum to 1, verify: $$ \sum_{i=1}^{N} P_i = \sum_{i=1}^{N} \frac{f_i}{\sum_{j=1}^{N} f_j} = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{N} f_i}{\sum_{j=1}^{N} f_j} = 1 \quad \checkmark $$ **Step 5:** The expected number of copies of individual *i* in the next generation is *N* times its selection probability. $$ E_i = N \cdot P_i = \frac{N \cdot f_i}{\bar{f}} \quad \text{where} \quad \bar{f} = \frac{F_{\text{total}}}{N} $$ --- ### 3.2 Derivation: Binary Encoding — Binary to Real Conversion **Problem:** Given a binary string $b = (b_1, b_2, \dots, b_L)$, convert it to a real value $x \in [L_b, U_b]$. **Step 1:** Compute the decimal equivalent of the binary string. $$ D = \sum_{k=1}^{L} b_k \cdot 2^{L-k} $$ **Step 2:** Apply linear interpolation to map the decimal range $[0, 2^L - 1]$ to the real interval $[L_b, U_b]$. $$ x = L_b + \frac{D}{2^L - 1} \cdot (U_b - L_b) $$ **Worked Example:** Let $L = 5$, $b = (1, 0, 1, 1, 0)$, $L_b = -2$, $U_b = 2$. $$ D = 1 \cdot 16 + 0 \cdot 8 + 1 \cdot 4 + 1 \cdot 2 + 0 \cdot 1 = 22 $$ $$ x = -2 + \frac{22}{31} \cdot (2 - (-2)) = -2 + \frac{22}{31} \cdot 4 $$ $$ x = -2 + 2.8387 = 0.8387 $$ --- ### 3.3 Derivation: Holland's Schema Theorem **Given:** A schema *H* with order *o(H)* and defining length *δ(H)* in a population of size *N*. **Goal:** Find a lower bound on the expected number of instances $m(H, t+1)$ in the next generation. **Step 1:** Under selection alone (no crossover/mutation), the expected count is: $$ E_S = m(H, t) \cdot \frac{f(H)}{\bar{f}} $$ This is because schema *H* survives with probability proportional to its average fitness ratio. **Step 2:** Apply crossover disruption. A schema survives single-point crossover with probability: $$ P_{\text{survive, crossover}} = 1 - p_c \cdot \frac{\delta(H)}{L - 1} $$ This assumes the cut point falls inside the schema's defining length (proportional to *δ(H)/(L-1)*). **Step 3:** Apply mutation disruption. Each fixed bit mutates with probability $p_m$. Survival probability is: $$ P_{\text{survive, mutation}} = (1 - p_m)^{o(H)} \approx 1 - p_m \cdot o(H) $$ **Step 4:** Combine all three operators (assuming independence): $$ E[m(H, t+1)] \geq m(H, t) \cdot \frac{f(H)}{\bar{f}} \cdot \left[1 - p_c \cdot \frac{\delta(H)}{L - 1} - p_m \cdot o(H)\right] $$ **Interpretation:** Short, low-order, above-average-fitness schemata grow exponentially — this is the foundation of GA's search power. --- ### 3.4 Worked KTU-Style Numerical Example > [!NOTE] > **Problem:** Maximize $f(x) = x^2$ for $x \in [0, 31]$, using $L = 5$ bits, $N = 4$. Initial population: $x_1 = 13$, $x_2 = 24$, $x_3 = 8$, $x_4 = 19$. Apply one generation of roulette-wheel selection followed by single-point crossover (cut at $k = 2$) and bit-flip mutation ($p_m = 0.05$). Compute the new population. **Step 1: Compute Fitness Values** $$ f(x_1) = 13^2 = 169, \quad f(x_2) = 24^2 = 576, \quad f(x_3) = 8^2 = 64, \quad f(x_4) = 19^2 = 361 $$ **Step 2: Compute Total and Average Fitness** $$ F_{\text{total}} = 169 + 576 + 64 + 361 = 1170 $$ $$ \bar{f} = \frac{1170}{4} = 292.5 $$ **Step 3: Compute Selection Probabilities and Expected Counts** $$ P_1 = \frac{169}{1170} = 0.1444, \quad E_1 = 4 \cdot 0.1444 = 0.578 $$ $$ P_2 = \frac{576}{1170} = 0.4923, \quad E_2 = 4 \cdot 0.4923 = 1.969 $$ $$ P_3 = \frac{64}{1170} = 0.0547, \quad E_3 = 4 \cdot 0.0547 = 0.219 $$ $$ P_4 = \frac{361}{1170} = 0.3085, \quad E_4 = 4 \cdot 0.3085 = 1.234 $$ **Step 4: Select Mating Pool (Roulette Wheel Sampling)** Assume the sampling yields: $x_2 = 24$ twice, $x_4 = 19$ once, $x_1 = 13$ once (based on $E_i$). So mating pool = $\{24, 24, 19, 13\}$. Binary representations (5-bit): $$ 24 = 11000, \quad 19 = 10011, \quad 13 = 01101 $$ **Step 5: Apply Single-Point Crossover at k = 2** Pair 1: $24 \otimes 19 = 11000 \otimes 10011$ $$ \text{Offspring 1: } 11 \vert 011 = 11011 = 27 $$ $$ \text{Offspring 2: } 10 \vert 000 = 10000 = 16 $$ Pair 2: $24 \otimes 13 = 11000 \otimes 01101$ $$ \text{Offspring 3: } 11 \vert 101 = 11101 = 29 $$ $$ \text{Offspring 4: } 01 \vert 000 = 01000 = 8 $$ **Step 6: Apply Bit-Flip Mutation ($p_m = 0.05$)** Assume no bits are flipped (probabilistic — assume $U(0,1) > 0.05$ for all bits). New population: $\{27, 16, 29, 8\}$. **Step 7: New Fitness Values** $$ f(27) = 729, \quad f(16) = 256, \quad f(29) = 841, \quad f(8) = 64 $$ $$ F_{\text{total,new}} = 1890, \quad \bar{f}_{\text{new}} = 472.5 $$ > **Observation:** Average fitness jumped from **292.5 → 472.5**, demonstrating GA's convergence behavior. This is the type of generation-by-generation numerical walk KTU examiners love to test. ## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Mermaid Flowchart: Complete Genetic Algorithm Cycle ```mermaid flowchart TD A([Start]) --> B[Initialize Population
N random chromosomes] B --> C[Decode Each Chromosome
to Real Variables] C --> D[Evaluate Fitness
f xi for all i] D --> E{Termination
Criterion Met?} E -- No --> F[Selection Operator
Roulette or Tournament] F --> G[Crossover with
probability pc] G --> H[Mutation with
probability pm] H --> I[Form New
Generation t plus 1] I --> D E -- Yes --> J([Output Best
Solution x star]) ``` ### 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram: Functional Architecture of GA Operators ```mermaid flowchart LR P1[Parent Chromosome 1] --> S[Selection Module] P2[Parent Chromosome 2] --> S S --> CX[Crossover Operator
pc control] CX --> OF1[Offspring 1] CX --> OF2[Offspring 2] OF1 --> MT[Mutation Operator
pm control] OF2 --> MT MT --> FIT[Fitness Evaluation
Module] FIT --> REP[Replacement Strategy
Generational or Steady State] REP --> POP[Updated Population] POP -. Next Iteration .-> P1 POP -. Next Iteration .-> P2 ``` ### 4.3 Mermaid Diagram: Crossover Operator Variants ```mermaid flowchart TD CO[Crossover Operator] --> SP[Single Point
Cut at k] CO --> TP[Two Point
Cuts at k1 and k2] CO --> UN[Uniform
Per bit coin flip] CO --> AR[Arithmetic
Weighted average] SP --> OFX[Offspring Produced] TP --> OFX UN --> OFX AR --> OFX ``` ### 4.4 Mermaid Diagram: Selection Pressure Comparison ```mermaid flowchart LR SEL[Selection Pressure Axis] --> RW[Roulette Wheel
Low Pressure] SEL --> RB[Rank Based
Medium Pressure] SEL --> TR[Tournament k=2
High Pressure] SEL --> TC[Tournament k=5
Very High Pressure] SEL --> TN[Truncation
Extreme Pressure] ``` > [!NOTE] > **Note to KTU Students:** Examiners frequently ask to **draw the GA flowchart** in Module 4. Memorize the 5-block flow: Initialize → Fitness → Selection → Crossover → Mutation → Loop. Label probabilities $p_c$, $p_m$ and termination conditions clearly for full marks. ### 4.5 Block-Level Functional Architecture: GA in Engineering Optimization Loop ```mermaid flowchart TD ENG[Engineering Problem] --> FORM[Mathematical Formulation
Decision Variables and Objective] FORM --> ENC[Encoding Strategy
Binary or Real Coded] ENC --> GA[Genetic Algorithm Core] GA --> SEL[Selection Operator] SEL --> XOV[Crossover Operator] XOV --> MUT[Mutation Operator] MUT --> DEC[Decoding and
Fitness Evaluation] DEC --> COND{Convergence
Achieved?} COND -- No --> SEL COND -- Yes --> OUT[Optimal Design
Parameters] OUT --> VAL[Engineering Validation
via Simulation or Test] VAL --> DEP[Deploy Solution] ``` ## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ### **PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)** #### **Question 1 (3 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **Q: Define Genetic Algorithm. List any four terminologies used in GA with their biological meaning.** **Model Answer:** > A **Genetic Algorithm** is a population-based, stochastic search heuristic inspired by **Darwin's theory of natural selection** and genetic evolution. It maintains a population of candidate solutions and iteratively applies selection, crossover, and mutation to evolve optimal or near-optimal solutions. Four key terminologies: | GA Term | Biological Meaning | |---|---| | Chromosome | A DNA strand encoding a candidate solution | | Population | Group of organisms/solutions in a generation | | Fitness | Reproductive success / quality measure | | Crossover | Genetic recombination during reproduction | | Mutation | Random genetic alteration | *(Any four suffice for 3 marks)* --- #### **Question 2 (3 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **Q: Differentiate between Crossover and Mutation in Genetic Algorithms.** **Model Answer:** | Parameter | Crossover | Mutation | |---|---|---| | **Purpose** | Combines good traits from two parents | Maintains diversity, explores new regions | | **Probability** | High (p_c ≈ 0.6 to 0.95) | Low (p_m ≈ 0.001 to 0.05) | | **Operation** | Swaps segments between two chromosomes | Flips individual bits randomly | | **Involves** | Two parent chromosomes | One chromosome (operates per bit) | | **Biological analogy** | Sexual reproduction | Random genetic copying error | | **Effect on schema** | Disrupts long schemata | Disrupts all fixed-position bits | --- ### **PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice** --- #### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **Q: (a)** Explain the fundamental steps of a Genetic Algorithm with a neat flowchart. Discuss the role of each operator. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** Consider the problem of maximizing $f(x) = x^2$ for $x \in [0, 31]$. Use a binary encoding of length 5 and a population size of 4. The initial population is $\{x_1, x_2, x_3, x_4\} = \{13, 24, 8, 19\}$. Perform **one generation** of the GA: compute fitness, apply **roulette-wheel selection**, perform **single-point crossover at position k = 3**, and **bit-flip mutation** with $p_m = 0.1$. Show the new population and comment on convergence. **(7 Marks)** --- ### **Model Solution to Question A:** #### **Part (a) — GA Fundamentals and Flowchart (7 Marks)** A Genetic Algorithm solves optimization problems by evolving a population of candidate solutions through biologically inspired operators. The five canonical phases are: **Step 1: Initialization** — Generate *N* random chromosomes (typically $N \in [50, 200]$). Each chromosome encodes a candidate solution using binary, real-valued, or permutation encoding. **Step 2: Fitness Evaluation** — Decode each chromosome and compute the objective function value. For maximization, $f_i = f(x_i)$. For minimization, apply a transformation to avoid negative fitness values. **Step 3: Selection** — Probabilistically select parents proportional to fitness. Roulette wheel, tournament selection, and rank-based methods are common. **Role:** Drives the population toward high-fitness regions. **Step 4: Crossover (with probability $p_c$)** — Recombine pairs of parents to produce offspring. **Role:** Exploits successful genetic combinations (building blocks). **Step 5: Mutation (with probability $p_m$)** — Randomly flip bits in offspring. **Role:** Maintains diversity, prevents premature convergence to local optima. The cycle repeats until a termination criterion (max generations, fitness threshold, or convergence) is met. **Flowchart:** ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Start] --> B[Initialize Population] B --> C[Evaluate Fitness] C --> D{Convergence?} D -- No --> E[Selection] E --> F[Crossover with pc] F --> G[Mutation with pm] G --> H[New Generation] H --> C D -- Yes --> I[Output Best Solution] ``` --- #### **Part (b) — Numerical GA Execution (7 Marks)** **Step 1: Compute Fitness Values** $$ f(x_1) = 13^2 = 169, \quad f(x_2) = 24^2 = 576 $$ $$ f(x_3) = 8^2 = 64, \quad f(x_4) = 19^2 = 361 $$ **Step 2: Total and Average Fitness** $$ F_{\text{total}} = 169 + 576 + 64 + 361 = 1170, \quad \bar{f} = 292.5 $$ **Step 3: Roulette Wheel Selection Probabilities** $$ P_1 = \frac{169}{1170} \approx 0.1444, \quad P_2 = \frac{576}{1170} \approx 0.4923 $$ $$ P_3 = \frac{64}{1170} \approx 0.0547, \quad P_4 = \frac{361}{1170} \approx 0.3085 $$ **Expected counts:** $E_1 = 0.578$, $E_2 = 1.969$, $E_3 = 0.219$, $E_4 = 1.234$. **Step 4: Mating Pool (Roulette Wheel Sampling)** Simulated sampling selects: $x_2 = 24$ (twice), $x_4 = 19$ (once), $x_1 = 13$ (once). Mating pool = $\{24, 24, 19, 13\}$. 5-bit binary: $11000$, $11000$, $10011$, $01101$. **Step 5: Single-Point Crossover at k = 3** Pair 1: $11000 \otimes 10011$ $$ \text{Offspring 1: } 110 \vert 11 = 11011 = 27 $$ $$ \text{Offspring 2: } 100 \vert 00 = 10000 = 16 $$ Pair 2: $11000 \otimes 01101$ $$ \text{Offspring 3: } 110 \vert 01 = 11101 = 29 $$ $$ \text{Offspring 4: } 011 \vert 00 = 01100 = 12 $$ **Step 6: Bit-Flip Mutation (p_m = 0.1)** Assume $U(0,1) > 0.1$ for all bits (no mutation occurs in this run). New population: $\{27, 16, 29, 12\}$. **Step 7: New Fitness** $$ f(27) = 729, \quad f(16) = 256, \quad f(29) = 841, \quad f(12) = 144 $$ $$ F_{\text{total,new}} = 1970, \quad \bar{f}_{\text{new}} = 492.5 $$ **Step 8: Convergence Comment** The average fitness improved from **292.5 → 492.5** (a **68.4% increase**) in a single generation. The maximum fitness $f(29) = 841$ is significantly higher than the original $f(24) = 576$. This demonstrates GA's rapid convergence behavior and validates the Building Block Hypothesis. The optimal $x^* = 31$ gives $f(x^*) = 961$, which the GA is approaching through evolutionary pressure. --- ### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **Q: (a)** Explain Roulette Wheel Selection with a suitable example. Derive the formula for the probability of selecting an individual. Discuss its limitations. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** State and prove **Holland's Schema Theorem**. Explain its significance in the working of Genetic Algorithms. **(7 Marks)** --- ### **Model Solution to Question B:** #### **Part (a) — Roulette Wheel Selection (7 Marks)** **Concept:** In Roulette Wheel Selection, each individual is assigned a slice of a circular "wheel" proportional to its fitness. A pointer is spun, and the individual in the slice where the pointer stops is selected. Higher fitness → larger slice → higher selection probability. **Derivation:** Total fitness: $F_{\text{total}} = \sum_{j=1}^{N} f_j$ Probability of individual *i*: $$ P_i = \frac{f_i}{\sum_{j=1}^{N} f_j} $$ **Verification:** $\sum P_i = 1$ (probabilities are valid). **Worked Example:** Population with fitness $\{10, 30, 20, 40\}$. $$ F_{\text{total}} = 100 $$ $$ P_1 = 0.10, \quad P_2 = 0.30, \quad P_3 = 0.20, \quad P_4 = 0.40 $$ Individual 4 has the largest slice and is most likely to be selected. **Limitations of Roulette Wheel Selection:** 1. **Negative fitness problem** — Cannot directly handle negative $f_i$ values. 2. **Premature convergence** — A super-fit individual can dominate the wheel early, causing loss of diversity. 3. **Slow convergence near optimum** — Selection pressure drops as fitness variance shrinks. 4. **No tunable pressure** — Pressure is dictated entirely by raw fitness ratios. --- #### **Part (b) — Holland's Schema Theorem (7 Marks)** **Statement:** Short, low-order, above-average-fitness schemata receive exponentially increasing trials in subsequent generations. **Proof:** Let $m(H, t)$ be the number of instances of schema *H* at generation *t*, and $f(H)$ its average fitness. **Step 1: Selection Operator** A schema *H* is selected with probability proportional to $f(H) / \bar{f}$. Expected count after selection: $$ E_S = m(H, t) \cdot \frac{f(H)}{\bar{f}} $$ **Step 2: Crossover Disruption** For single-point crossover, the schema is destroyed if the cut point falls between its outermost fixed bits. Defining length $\delta(H)$: $$ P_{\text{destroy, crossover}} = \frac{\delta(H)}{L - 1} $$ Survival probability: $$ P_{\text{survive, crossover}} \geq 1 - p_c \cdot \frac{\delta(H)}{L - 1} $$ **Step 3: Mutation Disruption** Each of the $o(H)$ fixed bits can mutate. Survival probability: $$ P_{\text{survive, mutation}} = (1 - p_m)^{o(H)} \geq 1 - p_m \cdot o(H) $$ **Step 4: Combining All Effects** $$ E[m(H, t+1)] \geq m(H, t) \cdot \frac{f(H)}{\bar{f}} \cdot \left[1 - p_c \cdot \frac{\delta(H)}{L - 1} - p_m \cdot o(H)\right] $$ If $f(H) > \bar{f}$, and $\delta(H)$, $o(H)$ are small, the bracket exceeds 1, and $m(H, t)$ grows exponentially. **Significance:** - Provides the **theoretical foundation** explaining why GAs work. - Justifies the **Building Block Hypothesis**: short, useful genetic patterns (building blocks) are sampled and recombined exponentially to form optimal solutions. - Guides GA parameter tuning: keep $\delta(H)$ and $o(H)$ small, prefer high $f(H)$. --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:** > > 1. **Confusing $p_c$ and $p_m$ roles:** Students frequently state that mutation is the primary search operator — **WRONG**. Crossover is the primary *exploitation* operator; mutation is for *exploration*. > 2. **Sign errors in fitness transformation:** When converting minimization to maximization, forgetting the absolute value or constant shift leads to negative roulette probabilities. > 3. **Schema notation error:** Using $\delta(H) = o(H)$ or confusing defining length with order. **Remember:** $o(H)$ = number of fixed bits, $\delta(H)$ = distance between outermost fixed bits. > 4. **Skipping the flowchart:** In 7-mark questions asking for GA steps, a diagram is **mandatory**. Students who skip it lose 2 marks easily. > 5. **Bit-length mismatch:** Forgetting to verify $L$ matches the precision required for the variable range. Use $2^L - 1 \geq (U_b - L_b) / \epsilon$. > 6. **Not showing binary-decimal conversions in numericals:** Examiners deduct marks if the binary-to-real decoding step is skipped. --- ### **Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember** > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — Genetic Algorithms** - **Definition:** GA is a population-based, stochastic, evolutionary metaheuristic inspired by natural selection. - **Core Loop (5 Phases):** Initialize → Fitness Evaluation → Selection → Crossover → Mutation → Loop. - **Encoding:** Binary (most common in KTU exams), real-valued, or permutation. - **Decoding formula:** $x = L_b + \dfrac{D}{2^L - 1} (U_b - L_b)$, where $D$ is the binary decimal value. - **Roulette Wheel Probability:** $P_i = \dfrac{f_i}{\sum_{j=1}^{N} f_j}$. - **Expected Count:** $E_i = N \cdot P_i = \dfrac{N \cdot f_i}{\bar{f}}$. - **Crossover types:** Single-point, Two-point, Uniform, Arithmetic (real-coded). - **Mutation:** Bit-flip (binary) or Gaussian (real-coded). - **Typical parameter ranges:** $N \in [50, 200]$, $p_c \in [0.6, 0.95]$, $p_m \in [0.001, 0.05]$. - **Schema Theorem:** Short, low-order, high-fitness schemata grow exponentially — the Building Block Hypothesis. - **Schema Order** $o(H)$: number of fixed positions. - **Defining Length** $\delta(H)$: last(H) − first(H). - **Termination:** Max generations, convergence ($\vert f_{\max} - \bar{f} \vert < \epsilon$), or fitness target reached. - **Advantages:** Global search, no gradient needed, handles discrete/combinatorial problems. - **Disadvantages:** Slow convergence, many parameters to tune, no guarantee of global optimum. - **Applications:** VLSI design, structural optimization, job-shop scheduling, neural network training, route planning, PID tuning. - **Numerical walkthrough format expected in KTU:** Decode → Fitness → Selection Probabilities → Crossover → Mutation → New Fitness → Comment on convergence.

topics.title: Simulated Annealing
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEECT637 - OPTIMIZATION TECHNIQUES
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Simulated Annealing

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Simulated Annealing (SA)** is a probabilistic metaheuristic optimization technique inspired by the **annealing process in metallurgy**, used to find an approximate global optimum of a given objective function in a large search space. It is characterized by a **stochastic neighborhood search** guided by a control parameter called **temperature**, which is gradually reduced according to a **cooling schedule**.

Mathematically, SA generates a sequence of candidate solutions $x^{(0)}, x^{(1)}, x^{(2)}, \ldots$ in the search space $\mathcal{S}$, where each new candidate $x'$ is drawn from a neighborhood $\mathcal{N}(x)$. The transition from $x$ to $x'$ is governed by the **Metropolis acceptance criterion**, allowing the algorithm to probabilistically escape local optima.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Metallurgy Connection

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Analogy: The Jeweler's Craft**
>
> Imagine a **jeweler heating a metal bracelet** to high temperature (so atoms can move freely) and then **slowly cooling it**. If cooled too fast, the metal hardens with internal stresses and defects (a *local, imperfect* structure). If cooled slowly enough, atoms settle into a near-perfect **crystalline arrangement** (the *global* best structure).
>
> Simulated Annealing mimics this exactly:
> - **Temperature $T$** $\;\longleftrightarrow\;$ thermal energy of atoms.
> - **Solution state $x$** $\;\longleftrightarrow\;$ atomic configuration of the metal.
> - **Cost $f(x)$** $\;\longleftrightarrow\;$ internal energy $E$ of the configuration.
> - **Cooling schedule** $\;\longleftrightarrow\;$ controlled slow cooling.

**Key Insight:** At high $T$, the algorithm freely explores "bad" regions; at low $T$, it behaves like a greedy local search, refining the best-so-far solution.

## 1.3 Why Do We Need Simulated Annealing?

Gradient-based and greedy algorithms suffer from one critical flaw: **they get trapped in local optima**.

| Method | Behaviour at Local Optimum | Probability of Escape |
|---|---|---|
| Steepest Descent | Stops permanently | $0$ |
| Random Restart | Only at restart | Low |
| Genetic Algorithms | Through crossover/mutation | Moderate |
| **Simulated Annealing** | **Probabilistic uphill moves** | **$e^{-\Delta E / T} > 0$** |

## 1.4 Salient Features

- **Probabilistic:** Uses randomness — results vary across runs.
- **Single-solution based:** Tracks *one* current solution (unlike GA's population).
- **Memoryless:** Decisions depend only on current state and $T$.
- **Asymptotically convergent:** Under slow cooling ($T(t) \geq C/\log(t+1)$), it is **proven to converge to the global optimum** with probability $1$ (Geman & Geman, 1984).
- **Black-box friendly:** Requires only a cost function $f(x)$, not its derivatives.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Underlying Principle — Statistical Mechanics

SA is grounded in the **Boltzmann distribution** from statistical mechanics. At thermal equilibrium at temperature $T$, the probability that a system is in a state of energy $E$ is:

$$
P(E) \;=\; \frac{1}{Z(T)} \, e^{-E \,/\, k_B T}
$$

where $k_B$ is the **Boltzmann constant** and $Z(T) = \sum_i e^{-E_i / k_B T}$ is the **partition function**.

> [!NOTE]
> In SA, we **drop the constant $k_B$** (absorbed into the temperature definition) and rename energy as the **objective (cost) value $f(x)$**. Hence:
>
> $$P(x) \;\propto\; e^{-f(x)\,/\,T}$$

This means: **lower-cost solutions are always more probable**, but at high $T$, even high-cost states have non-negligible probability.

## 2.2 The Metropolis Acceptance Criterion

Given the current solution $x$ with cost $f(x)$ and a neighbor $x'$ with cost $f(x')$, define the **energy change**:

$$
\Delta E \;=\; f(x') \;-\; f(x)
$$

The Metropolis criterion (1953) decides whether to move to $x'$:

$$
P_{\text{accept}}(x \to x') \;=\;
\begin{cases}
1, & \Delta E \leq 0 \quad (\text{better or equal solution}) \\[4pt]
e^{-\Delta E \,/\, T}, & \Delta E > 0 \quad (\text{worse solution})
\end{cases}
$$

## 2.3 The Cooling Schedule

A cooling schedule is a rule $T_{k+1} = \mathcal{C}(T_k)$ governing how temperature decreases. KTU frequently tests the following:

| Schedule | Formula | Behaviour |
|---|---|---|
| **Geometric (exponential)** | $T_{k+1} = \alpha \cdot T_k$, $\;0 < \alpha < 1$ | Most common; $\alpha = 0.9$ to $0.99$ |
| **Linear** | $T_{k+1} = T_k - \beta$ | Simple, can cool too fast |
| **Logarithmic** | $T_k = C / \log(k + 1)$ | Geman–Geman optimal, slow in practice |
| **Adaptive** | $T_{k+1}$ depends on $\Delta f$ history | Sophisticated, problem-specific |

## 2.4 Termination Conditions

Common stopping criteria:

- Temperature falls below threshold: $T_k < T_{\min}$
- Maximum number of iterations reached
- No improvement in best solution for $N_{\text{stale}}$ consecutive iterations
- Cost function value below target $f_{\text{target}}$

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| # | Formula | Description | Notation |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $P(E_i) = \dfrac{1}{Z} e^{-E_i / k_B T}$ | Boltzmann equilibrium distribution | $Z$ partition function |
| 2 | $\Delta E = f(x') - f(x)$ | Energy (cost) change on transition | $f$ objective |
| 3 | $P_{\text{acc}} = e^{-\Delta E / T}$ when $\Delta E > 0$ | Metropolis acceptance | $T$ current temperature |
| 4 | $P_{\text{acc}} = 1$ when $\Delta E \leq 0$ | Greedy acceptance for improvement | — |
| 5 | $T_{k+1} = \alpha \cdot T_k$ | Geometric cooling | $0 < \alpha < 1$ |
| 6 | $T_k = \dfrac{C}{\log(k+1)}$ | Logarithmic cooling (Geman–Geman) | $C > 0$ |
| 7 | $P(\text{global opt}) \to 1$ as $k \to \infty$ | Convergence guarantee | under slow cooling |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In SA literature, the **vertical bar** in conditional probability notation must NEVER use the markdown-table-breaking pipe symbol. Write it as $\,\mid\,$ or as $\,\vert\,$ inside equations.

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Field | Application |
|---|---|
| **VLSI Design** | Optimal placement of components on a chip |
| **Logistics** | Travelling Salesman Problem (TSP) for route planning |
| **Image Processing** | Image segmentation, noise removal |
| **Machine Learning** | Hyperparameter tuning, neural architecture search |
| **Structural Engineering** | Truss topology optimization |
| **Telecommunications** | Optimal routing in packet-switched networks |

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Metropolis Acceptance Probability from Boltzmann Distribution

**Goal:** Show why $P_{\text{acc}} = e^{-\Delta E / T}$ when $\Delta E > 0$.

### Step 1 — State the equilibrium distribution

At thermal equilibrium, the probability of finding the system in microstate $i$ with energy $E_i$ is given by the canonical (Boltzmann) distribution:

$$
P_{\text{eq}}(E_i) \;=\; \frac{e^{-E_i / k_B T}}{\sum_{j} e^{-E_j / k_B T}} \;=\; \frac{1}{Z(T)}\, e^{-E_i / k_B T}
$$

The denominator $Z(T)$ is the **partition function** which normalises the distribution.

### Step 2 — Form the ratio between two candidate states

Consider two configurations: the current state $x$ with cost $f(x)$ and a candidate neighbor $x'$ with cost $f(x')$. The ratio of their equilibrium probabilities is:

$$
\frac{P_{\text{eq}}(x')}{P_{\text{eq}}(x)} \;=\; \frac{\dfrac{1}{Z} e^{-f(x')/T}}{\dfrac{1}{Z} e^{-f(x)/T}} \;=\; e^{-(f(x')-f(x))/T} \;=\; e^{-\Delta E / T}
$$

The partition function $Z$ cancels out because both states share the same search space.

### Step 3 — Apply the detailed-balance condition

For the Markov chain to converge to the Boltzmann distribution, **detailed balance** must hold at equilibrium:

$$
P_{\text{eq}}(x) \cdot P(x \to x') \;=\; P_{\text{eq}}(x') \cdot P(x' \to x)
$$

Assuming a **symmetric proposal distribution** (i.e., probability of proposing $x'$ from $x$ equals probability of proposing $x$ from $x'$), the proposal probabilities cancel, giving:

$$
\frac{P(x \to x')}{P(x' \to x)} \;=\; \frac{P_{\text{eq}}(x')}{P_{\text{eq}}(x)} \;=\; e^{-\Delta E / T}
$$

### Step 4 — Construct the acceptance rule

If $\Delta E \leq 0$, the proposal is at least as good as the current — set $P_{\text{acc}} = 1$ (always move).

If $\Delta E > 0$, the proposal is worse — set $P_{\text{acc}}(x \to x') = e^{-\Delta E / T}$ so detailed balance is preserved.

$$
\boxed{\; P_{\text{acc}}(x \to x') \;=\; \min\!\Bigl\{\, 1,\; e^{-\Delta E / T} \,\Bigr\} \;}
$$

This compact form captures both cases and is the canonical Metropolis criterion used in SA.

---

## 3.2 Convergence Statement (Geman & Geman Theorem, 1984)

If the cooling schedule satisfies:

$$
T_k \;\geq\; \frac{C}{\log(k+1)} \quad \text{for all } k \geq 1
$$

for some constant $C$ larger than the maximum depth of any local (non-global) minimum, then the Markov chain generated by SA converges in distribution to the set of global minima:

$$
\lim_{k \to \infty} P\!\bigl(x^{(k)} \in \mathcal{S}^{*}\bigr) \;=\; 1
$$

where $\mathcal{S}^{*}$ denotes the set of globally optimal solutions.

---

## 3.3 Exhaustive Numerical Worked Example (Step-by-Step Trace)

**Problem:** Minimize $f(x) = x^2$ over the discrete set $x \in \{-3, -2, -1, 0, 1, 2, 3\}$.

**Parameters:**
- Initial solution: $x^{(0)} = 3$
- Initial temperature: $T_0 = 10$
- Cooling schedule: $T_{k+1} = 0.5 \cdot T_k$
- Neighbourhood: $\mathcal{N}(x) = \{x-1,\, x+1\}$ (clipped to $[-3,\,3]$)
- Acceptance rule: $P_{\text{acc}} = 1$ if $\Delta E \leq 0$, else $e^{-\Delta E / T}$
- Random draws for uphill moves (fixed sequence for reproducibility): $r = 0.50,\, 0.95,\, 0.80,\, 0.60,\, 0.40$

### Iteration Trace Matrix

| Iter $k$ | $T_k$ | Current $x$ | $f(x)$ | Trial $x'$ | $f(x')$ | $\Delta E$ | $P_{\text{acc}}$ | $r$ | Decision | New $x$ |
|---:|---:|---:|---:|---:|---:|---:|---:|:---:|:---:|---:|
| 1 | 10.0 | 3 | 9 | 2 | 4 | $-5$ | $1$ | — | **accept** | 2 |
| 2 | 10.0 | 2 | 4 | 1 | 1 | $-3$ | $1$ | — | **accept** | 1 |
| 3 | 10.0 | 1 | 1 | 0 | 0 | $-1$ | $1$ | — | **accept** | 0 |
| 4 | 10.0 | 0 | 0 | $-1$ | 1 | $+1$ | $e^{-1/10}=0.9048$ | 0.50 | **accept** | $-1$ |
| 5 | 10.0 | $-1$ | 1 | 0 | 0 | $-1$ | $1$ | — | **accept** | 0 |
| — | — | — | — | — | — | — | — | — | *cool*: $T=5.0$ | — |
| 6 | 5.0 | 0 | 0 | 1 | 1 | $+1$ | $e^{-1/5}=0.8187$ | 0.95 | **reject** | 0 |
| 7 | 5.0 | 0 | 0 | $-1$ | 1 | $+1$ | $0.8187$ | 0.60 | **accept** | $-1$ |
| 8 | 5.0 | $-1$ | 1 | 0 | 0 | $-1$ | $1$ | — | **accept** | 0 |
| — | — | — | — | — | — | — | — | — | *cool*: $T=2.5$ | — |
| 9 | 2.5 | 0 | 0 | 1 | 1 | $+1$ | $e^{-1/2.5}=0.6703$ | 0.80 | **reject** | 0 |
| 10 | 2.5 | 0 | 0 | $-1$ | 1 | $+1$ | $0.6703$ | 0.40 | **accept** | $-1$ |
| — | — | — | — | — | — | — | — | — | *cool*: $T=1.25$ | — |
| 11 | 1.25 | $-1$ | 1 | 0 | 0 | $-1$ | $1$ | — | **accept** | 0 |
| — | — | — | — | — | — | — | — | — | *cool*: $T=0.625$ | — |
| 12 | 0.625 | 0 | 0 | 1 | 1 | $+1$ | $e^{-1/0.625}=0.2019$ | 0.60 | **reject** | 0 |
| 13 | 0.625 | 0 | 0 | $-1$ | 1 | $+1$ | $0.2019$ | 0.40 | **reject** | 0 |
| — | — | — | — | — | — | — | — | — | *cool*: $T=0.3125$ | — |
| 14 | 0.3125 | 0 | 0 | 1 | 1 | $+1$ | $0.0432$ | 0.60 | **reject** | 0 |

### Observations from the Trace

1. **High $T$ (early):** Algorithm freely accepts uphill moves (iter 4, 7) — explores broadly.
2. **Low $T$ (late):** Algorithm rejects almost all uphill moves (iter 12, 13, 14) — behaves greedily.
3. **Global optimum** $f(x^{*}) = f(0) = 0$ is **reached at iteration 3** and preserved thereafter.
4. **Best-so-far** = $0$ at $x^{*}=0$.

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Algorithm Flowchart

The following Mermaid diagram traces the full control flow of the Simulated Annealing algorithm, including the two-branch acceptance decision.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    n1["Start: Initialize current solution x and temperature T0"]
    n2["Set best solution xBest = x, fBest = f(x)"]
    n3["Generate candidate xPrime from neighbourhood N(x)"]
    n4["Compute deltaE = f(xPrime) - f(x)"]
    n5{"deltaE is less than or equal to 0?"}
    n6["Move: set x = xPrime"]
    n7["Draw random r uniformly in 0 to 1"]
    n8{"r is less than exp(-deltaE divided by T)?"}
    n9["Move: set x = xPrime"]
    n10["Reject: keep x unchanged"]
    n11["Update xBest if f(x) is better than fBest"]
    n12{"Stopping criterion satisfied?"}
    n13["Apply cooling: T = cooling schedule of T"]
    n14["Output best solution xBest with cost fBest"]

    n1 --> n2 --> n3 --> n4 --> n5
    n5 -- yes --> n6 --> n11
    n5 -- no --> n7 --> n8
    n8 -- yes --> n9 --> n11
    n8 -- no --> n10 --> n11
    n11 --> n12
    n12 -- no --> n13 --> n3
    n12 -- yes --> n14
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture

This block diagram captures the **modular interactions** among the four core subsystems of SA: the *solution generator*, the *cost evaluator*, the *acceptance module*, and the *cooling controller*.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    b1["Input Problem Data and Initial Solution x"]
    b2["Module A: Solution Generator — produces neighbour xPrime"]
    b3["Module B: Cost Evaluator — computes f(x) and f(xPrime)"]
    b4["Module C: Acceptance Module — applies Metropolis criterion"]
    b5["Module D: Cooling Schedule Controller — updates T"]
    b6["Current Solution State x"]
    b7["Random Number Generator — uniform 0 to 1"]
    b8["Output: Best Solution xBest and Cost fBest"]

    b1 --> b6
    b6 --> b2
    b2 --> b3
    b3 --> b4
    b4 --> b6
    b4 --> b7
    b7 --> b4
    b6 --> b5
    b5 --> b4
    b5 --> b2
    b6 --> b8
```

## 4.3 Temperature-vs-Acceptance Behaviour

The following conceptual block diagram summarises the **qualitative behaviour of SA across the temperature axis**.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    c1["High T: Random Search — accepts almost all moves, behaves like uniform random sampling"]
    c2["Moderate T: Exploratory Phase — selectively accepts worse moves to escape local optima"]
    c3["Low T: Exploitative Phase — mostly greedy, accepts only improvements"]
    c4["T less than Tmin: Frozen — converges to final solution"]

    c1 --> c2 --> c3 --> c4
```
